## Where to resume now (session 56 legacy snapshot below)

**Project management is now Claude Cowork** (running on Alexander's
desktop). When Cowork opens this repo, this is the first section to
read; \texttt{PLAN.md} is the binding multi-surface roadmap.

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-56):} \textbf{930 /
958 works drafted (\~97.1\%)} --- session 56 opened the \textbf{45 BC
philosophica run} (still \textbf{Phase 3}, the major-works sweep) by
drafting the surviving \textit{Academica} (45 BC,
\texttt{045bc-academica}, \textbf{46 sections}, \~29 KB Latin) end to
end. Drafted count 929 $\rightarrow$ 930.

\textbf{Which \textit{Academica}:} Perseus \texttt{phi045} carries only
the surviving torso of the \emph{second} edition --- the dialogue called
\textbf{``the Varro''} (Academica Posteriora Book I), set at Cicero's
Cumanum among Cicero, Varro, and Atticus, breaking off mid-sentence at
\textsection 46 (the Carneades portrait). The companion
\textit{Lucullus} (the surviving book of the \emph{first} edition) is a
separate Perseus text, \emph{not} currently a works.yaml entry; flag for
a possible later addition if the bound volume wants the whole surviving
Academica. The works.yaml \texttt{notes} field garbles the two-edition
history (it swaps Priora/Posteriora and the speaker pairs) --- worth a
one-line correction in an apparatus pass, not blocking.

\textbf{Method (parallel, section-range):} PM pre-fetched the Latin
(\texttt{fetch\_latin.py academica}, clean Perseus content, 46
\texttt{ciceroSection} markers, no fallback dump), sliced it into
\textbf{four contiguous ranges} (\textsection\textsection 1--12,
13--24, 25--35, 36--46) written to the Cowork \emph{outputs} scratch
dir, and dispatched \textbf{four worker sub-agents in one parallel
batch} under \texttt{046bc-brutus} as the register/dialogue template
(English parts only). PM assembled the four English parts into
\texttt{english/philosophy/045bc-academica.tex} (\textbf{verified
contiguous \textsection\textsection 1--46}, header added on assembly;
\~36 KB), wrote the whole-work headnote itself, and flipped status.
Per-work parallel sidecar generated by
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (\textbf{46 segments, zero empty
eng/lat}).

\textbf{Metadata:} no correction needed --- \textit{Academica} is a
treatise with no Perseus dateline; the works.yaml date
(\texttt{-0045-05-01}, precision \texttt{month}) is consistent with the
standard mid-45 BC dating; left as-is.

\textbf{Greek handled:} workers rendered all Greek meaning-first with
\textit{[Greek: \ldots]}: the load-bearing epistemological coinage
Cicero forges in Latin --- \textit{qualitas}/\textit{poiotes}
(\textsection\textsection 24--25), the Platonic
\textit{idea}/\textit{species} (\textsection 30), \textit{etymologia}
(\textsection 32), \textit{phantasia} (\textsection 40, Cicero's
\textit{visum}), \textit{katal\=empton}/\textit{comprehendibile}
(\textsection 41, the Stoic ``graspable presentation''). The
\textsection 29 and \textsection 46 textual lacunae (Perseus
\texttt{* * *}) rendered as ellipses per the no-brackets-in-body rule.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging} (deferred to an enrichment pass
to avoid concurrent-write contention): the vocabulary-coinage thread is
the richest note-cluster --- \textit{visum} vs
``presentation''/``impression'' (\textsection\textsection 40--41),
\textit{comprehendibile}/\textit{comprehensio} rendered with the
hand-metaphor ``graspable''/``grasp'' rather than the cognate
``comprehension,'' \textit{praeposita}/\textit{reiecta} = Stoic
``preferred''/``dispreferred'' (\textsection 37), \textit{opinatio} vs
\textit{opinio} (\textsection\textsection 41--42, 45). A daggered crux
at \textsection 13 (\texttt{negaret}) and the corrupt \textsection 46
break (\texttt{...facultate et to}) were rendered for sense;
flag-not-resolve for a later Teubner/OCT check.

\textbf{Validation:} \texttt{validate.py} clean --- exit 0, 9 warnings
= the two orphan-Latin carry-overs (\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49}
and \texttt{046bc-ad-familiares-09-25}, both pre-existing) and the
chronological-gap warning, now at \textbf{22} pending earlier-dated
works (down from 23 post-session-55, the expected $-1$ since
\textit{Academica} falls before the deep-chronology frontier).
\textbf{Latest drafted by deep chronology unchanged}: \textit{Ad
Familiares} 10.24 (28 July 43 BC). \texttt{build\_manifest.py}
regenerated \texttt{MANIFEST.md} (958 entries) and
\texttt{build/chronology.tex}.

\textbf{Suggested next batch (session 57):} continue the 45 BC
philosophica run with \textbf{\textit{de-finibus}} ---
\textbf{already pre-fetched this session}
(\texttt{latin/philosophy/045bc-de-finibus.tex}, \~326 KB,
\textbf{443 sections} across five books). It is the longest treatise yet
in the sweep; \textbf{plan \~2 sessions}. Slice into \~10--14 contiguous
section-ranges, dispatch in batches of four under
\texttt{046bc-brutus}/\texttt{046bc-orator}/\texttt{045bc-academica} as
register templates; the PM assembles, verifies the section sequence, and
writes the whole-work headnote. Because it is multi-book,
\textbf{verify the book boundaries in the section numbering when
slicing} (Perseus may restart section numbers per book; if so, slice
along book lines and preserve per-book numbering). After
\textit{de-finibus}: \textit{tusculanae-disputationes} (45),
\textit{de-natura-deorum} (45), then \textit{de-officiis} (44).

trochee, iamb, dochmiac).

sections), and \textit{Paradoxa Stoicorum} (46 BC, 52 sections).
Drafted count 925 $\rightarrow$ 928.

\textbf{Method (parallel):} the PM pre-fetched the next four fetchable
works (the three above plus \textit{Orator}) via
\texttt{scripts/fetch\_latin.py}, then dispatched \textbf{four worker
sub-agents in one parallel batch} under \texttt{046bc-brutus} and
\texttt{046bc-ad-familiares-09-02} as register/voice templates: one
full end-to-end on the letter (English + headnote + parallel sidecar),
one full end-to-end on \textit{De Optimo} (English + headnote), and two
on \textit{Paradoxa} by section-range (\textsection\textsection 1--26 /
27--52, English parts only). The PM assembled the two \textit{Paradoxa}
parts into \texttt{english/philosophy/046bc-paradoxa-stoicorum.tex}
(\textbf{verified contiguous \textsection\textsection 1--52}, header
added on assembly), wrote the \textit{Paradoxa} whole-work headnote
itself, and flipped statuses. Per-work parallel sidecars generated by
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (de-optimo 23 segs, paradoxa 52 segs,
letter 3 segs; zero empty segments).

\textbf{Metadata correction (important --- a real chronology bug):}
\textbf{\textit{Ad Familiares} 9.25 was misdated.} The works.yaml entry
carried the book-level placeholder \texttt{-0046-07-01}
(\texttt{year\_range}) and pointed \texttt{latin\_source\_url} at
\texttt{thelatinlibrary.com} (whose earlier fetch had left a
\texttt{\% PLACEHOLDER} stub from session 18, so the work had never
actually been fetched). The Perseus dateline (\texttt{Scr. Laudiceae
post iii Id. Febr.; a. 704}) fixes the letter at \textbf{ca. 13
February 50 BC} --- it is the playful ``supreme commander'' letter to
Paetus written during Cicero's Cilician proconsulship (salutation
\textsc{Cicero Imp. Paeto}), \emph{four years earlier} than the rest of
Book 9, which is otherwise the 46 BC Paetus/Varro cluster. Fixes
applied: corrected the Perseus URL to \texttt{phi0474.phi056}; added
\texttt{speech\_index: book:9,letter:25}; corrected \texttt{date} to
\texttt{-0050-02-13} (precision \texttt{day}), set \texttt{recipient:
PAETO} and \texttt{location\_written: Laodiceae}; removed the
\texttt{date\_range\_end}; and \textbf{renamed the three files
\texttt{046bc-} $\rightarrow$ \texttt{050bc-}}. The old
\texttt{046bc-ad-familiares-09-25.tex} Latin was overwritten with a
stub and is now an orphan carry-over (handoff cleans it).

\textbf{Greek handled:} letter --- \textit{Paideia Kyrou} (Xenophon's
\textit{Cyropaedia}). \textit{Paradoxa} --- the six betacode
paradox-titles rendered meaning-first with \textit{[Greek: \ldots]}
(\textsection 6 \textit{hoti monon to kalon agathon}; \textsection 16
\textit{autark\=es h\=e aret\=e}; \textsection 20 \textit{isa ta
hamart\=emata}; \textsection 27 \textit{pas aphr\=on mainetai};
\textsection 33 \textit{monos ho sophos eleutheros}; \textsection 42
\textit{monos ho sophos plousios}), plus \textit{paradoxa} and
\textit{thetik\=os} in the proem. \textit{De Optimo} has no inline Greek
script (Attic figures named in Roman form).

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging} (deferred to a future
enrichment pass to avoid concurrent-write contention):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\textit{De Optimo} \textsection 14} --- the famous
translation-method statement (\textit{nec converti ut interpres sed ut
orator}; words ``weighed, not counted out like coins''), rendered fresh
per STYLE.md's no-echo rule, keeping Cicero's \textit{adnumerare /
appendere} coin image.
\item \textbf{\textit{Paradoxa} \textsection 27} --- a genuine lacuna
in the Perseus Latin, left as \texttt{* * *} in the English rather than
filled; \textsection 46 carries a daggered crux given a sense-rendering.
The register deliberately hardens into invective in the later paradoxes
(the demagogue of paradox IV, the grasping politician of paradox VI
standing in for Cicero's enemies).
\item \textbf{\textit{Fam} 9.25 \textsection 3} --- a stray ``is''
(likely OCR/textual noise) translated for sense.
\end{itemize}

## Where to resume now (session 53 legacy snapshot below)

**Project management is now Claude Cowork** (running on Alexander's
desktop). When Cowork opens this repo, this is the first section to
read; \texttt{PLAN.md} is the binding multi-surface roadmap.

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-53):} \textbf{925 /
958 works drafted (\~96.6\%)} --- session 53 continued \textbf{Phase 3
(the major-works sweep)} by translating the single chronologically-next
fetchable major work: \textit{Brutus} (46 BC), Cicero's history of Roman
oratory (311 sections present in Perseus's numbering 1--333,
$\sim$38{,}000 English words). With \textit{Pro Milone} done last session
and the two manual-source snags (\textit{de-legibus},
\textit{ad-familiares-05-10}) deliberately skipped, this was the next
non-deferred fetchable work; the chronology pointer now advances into the
46--44 BC rhetorica/philosophica run.

\textbf{Method (parallel, by section-range):} the PM pre-fetched
\textit{Brutus} via \texttt{scripts/fetch\_latin.py} (Perseus TEI
\texttt{phi0474.phi039}, 175{,}585 chars, 311 sections, clean 1--333 run
with the standard scholarly numbering gaps), sliced the Latin into twelve
contiguous range files in the Cowork \emph{outputs} scratch dir, and
dispatched twelve worker sub-agents in \textbf{three parallel batches of
four} (\textsection\textsection 1--26, 27--52, 53--81, 82--107 /
108--135, 136--163, 164--190, 191--219 / 220--248, 249--278, 279--305,
306--333), all under \texttt{055bc-de-oratore} as the register/voice
template. Each worker emitted only its English part file to scratch; the
PM assembled the twelve parts into
\texttt{english/rhetoric/046bc-brutus.tex} (header added on assembly),
\textbf{verified the assembled section sequence is identical to the
Latin's} (311 markers, exact 1--333 run with all gaps preserved --- one
spurious inline \textsection 282 marker introduced by the
\textsection\textsection 279--305 worker was caught in the marker-diff
check and removed), wrote the whole-work headnote itself (whole-work
understanding needed), and flipped the status. Per-work parallel sidecar
generated by \texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (311 segments, locs
1--333, zero empty segs).

\textbf{Date / Greek / register checks:} \textbf{No date correction} ---
\textit{Brutus} carries no Perseus dateline; the conventional early-46 BC
dating is retained (\texttt{date: -0046-04-01}, precision \texttt{month})
and the file is correctly named \texttt{046bc-}. \textbf{Eight Greek
phrases} occur and are rendered meaning-first with \textit{[Greek:
\ldots]} (\textit{Peith\=o} \textsection 59; \textit{tropoi} /
\textit{sch\=emata} \textsection 69; \textit{periodon} / \textit{k\=ola}
\textsection 162; \textit{politikon} \textsection 265; \textit{sch\=emata}
\textsection 275). Speaker turns (Cicero / Brutus / Atticus) are rendered
inline as narrative dialogue per the \texttt{de-oratore} convention, not
as \texttt{\textbackslash textsc\{\}} catechism labels.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (deferred to a future enrichment
pass to avoid concurrent-write contention):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\textsection\textsection 16--19} --- the sustained
debt/dunning conceit (\textit{flagitator}) for the ``gift'' of writing
Cicero owes Brutus; the financial metaphor kept running across the
sequence rather than flattened.
\item \textbf{\textsection\textsection 58--59} --- the Ennius
\textit{Annales} fragment on Cethegus (\textit{Suadae medulla}, ``the
marrow of Persuasion''), set as verse; \textit{Suada} kept as a proper
name beside the Greek gloss \textit{Peith\=o}.
\item \textbf{\textsection 160} --- \textit{Liciniam virginem} rendered
``the Vestal Licinia'' (interpretive expansion; the Latin says only ``the
maiden Licinia''). \textbf{Flag for editorial review} --- the volume may
prefer the literal.
\item \textbf{\textsection 192} --- \textit{scalmus} ``thole-pin,''
Crassus's witticism (a whole ship raised from one peg, mirroring Scaevola
building a whole suit from one quibble); literal nautical image kept.
\item \textbf{\textsection 260} --- \textit{sputatilica}, an
untranslatable coinage/pun, rendered ``spittable'' with the joke split
(``spit I know; -table I do not'') to keep Rusius's mockery legible.
\item \textbf{\textsection 283} --- \textit{verum sanguinem
deperdebat}, of Calvus's over-thinned style, ``kept losing even his true
blood''; the literal blood-image kept.
\item \textbf{\textsection\textsection 330--333} --- the closing
apostrophe to Brutus: the ``guardians of an orphaned eloquence'' allegory
kept its full image, and the text deliberately ends on the genuine broken
final sentence (\textit{si mihi accidisset\ldots}) rather than smoothed
into closure --- the work falls silent in the act of recording its own
history.
\item \textbf{Daggered textual cruxes} in Perseus (e.g.\ \textsection
46, \textsection 175, \textsection 206 ``Q. Metello \dag F.'',
\textsection 207 \dag Cotta\dag, \textsection 233 \dag admirando\dag,
\textsection 315) rendered through to the standard sense; obeli preserved
in the English where a name or word is genuinely lost.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Validation:} \texttt{validate.py} clean --- exit 0, 8 warnings =
the pre-existing orphan-Latin (\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49}) plus
the chronological-gap warning, now at \textbf{27} pending earlier-dated
works (down from 28 post-session-52, the expected $-1$ decrement since
\textit{Brutus} at 46 BC fell inside the chronological-gap zone).

Familiares} 10.24 (28 July 43 BC). \texttt{build\_manifest.py}
regenerated \texttt{MANIFEST.md} (958 entries) and
\texttt{build/chronology.tex}.

$\sim$15{,}560 English words). With \textit{Pro Plancio} done last
session, this was the next non-deferred pending work; the 52 BC
\textit{vis}-trial speech is now drafted and the chronology pointer
advances past it into a run of manual-source snags (see ``next batch''
below).

\textbf{Method (parallel, by section-range):} the PM pre-fetched
\textit{Pro Milone} via \texttt{scripts/fetch\_latin.py} (Perseus TEI
\texttt{phi0474.phi031}, 73{,}006 chars, 105 sections, clean 1..105
run), sliced the Latin into four contiguous range files in the Cowork
\emph{outputs} scratch dir, and dispatched four worker sub-agents in
one batch (\textsection\textsection 1--26, 27--53, 54--79, 80--105),
all under \texttt{054bc-pro-plancio} as the register/voice template.
Each worker emitted only its English part file to scratch; the PM
assembled the four parts into
\texttt{english/speeches/052bc-pro-milone.tex} (header added on
assembly, exact 1..105 sequence verified --- no gaps/dupes), wrote the
whole-speech headnote itself (whole-speech understanding needed), and
flipped the status. Per-work parallel sidecar was generated by
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (105 segments, locs 1--105, zero empty
English segs) rather than emitted by workers, to remove a
JSON-malformation risk; backfill aligns mechanically on
\texttt{\textbackslash ciceroSection} labels.

\textbf{Date / Greek / register checks:} \textbf{No date correction} ---
speeches carry no Perseus dateline; the conventional April 52 BC dating
is retained (\texttt{date: -0052-04-01}, precision \texttt{month}) and
the file is correctly named \texttt{052bc-}. \textbf{No Greek phrases}
occur anywhere in the speech. Oath/religion register checked against the
template: \textit{me hercule} $\rightarrow$ ``by Hercules,''
\textit{me dius fidius} $\rightarrow$ ``so help me god'' (lowercase,
matching \texttt{pro-plancio}), and the \textsection 85 apostrophe to
the Alban hills / Jupiter of Latium kept fully Roman (not
Christianized).

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (deferred to a future enrichment
pass to avoid concurrent-write contention):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\textsection 11} --- the famous \textit{silent enim leges
inter arma} rendered freshly as ``amid arms the laws fall silent''
(deliberately not echoing the canonical ``in time of war the laws fall
silent''); Cicero's word order kept so the antithesis lands where his
does.
\item \textbf{\textsection\textsection 10--11} --- \textit{non scripta
sed nata lex}, the unwritten/inborn natural-law passage; the asyndetic
tricola (\textit{adripuimus, hausimus, expressimus}; \textit{docti/facti,
instituti/imbuti}) preserved as English doublets to carry the build.
\item \textbf{\textsection 15} --- the ballot tablets A (\textit{absolvo})
/ C (\textit{condemno}) rendered ``letter of acquittal'' / ``that grim
one of condemnation''; literal ``letter'' would baffle a modern reader.
\textbf{Glossary candidate}: the Roman voting letters.
\item \textbf{\textsection 30} --- \textit{vi victa vis vel potius
oppressa virtute audacia est}, dense alliterative chiasmus, rendered
``force was conquered by force, or rather audacity was crushed by
valour'' (force/valour, force/audacity antithesis kept without forcing
English alliteration).
\item \textbf{\textsection 32} --- the Cassian \textit{cui bono} maxim
rendered as the recognizable principle ``to whose benefit,'' not left as
a bare Latin tag.
\item \textbf{\textsection 57} --- \textit{facti...quaestio est, iuris
in iudicio}: ``fact on the rack, law in court,'' \textit{eculeo}
(``little horse'' rack) kept as plain ``rack.''
\item \textbf{\textsection 105} --- \textit{O terram illam beatam}: the
asyndetic tricolon of conditional exclamations (\textit{exceperit /
eiecerit / amiserit}) preserved as a broken, suspended cadence rather
than smoothed into one sentence.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Validation:} \texttt{validate.py} clean --- 8 warnings = the
pre-existing orphan-Latin (\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49}) plus the
chronological-gap warning, now at \textbf{28} pending earlier-dated

**Project management is now Claude Cowork** (running on Alexander's
desktop). When Cowork opens this repo, this is the first section to
read; \texttt{PLAN.md} is the binding multi-surface roadmap.

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-51):} \textbf{923 /
958 works drafted (\~96.3\%)} --- session 51 continued \textbf{Phase 3
(the major-works sweep)} by clearing the three remaining 54 BC forensic
speeches in one parallel batch: \textit{Pro Plancio} (104 sections,
$\sim$12{,}900 English words), \textit{Pro Rabirio Postumo} (48
sections, $\sim$3{,}650 words), and the fragmentary \textit{Pro Scauro}
(76 surviving section-sigla, $\sim$2{,}930 words). These were the
chronologically-earliest non-deferred pending works; with them drafted,
the 54 BC tier of speeches is complete and the next non-deferred work
is \textit{Pro Milone} (52 BC).

\textbf{Method (parallel, mixed split):} the PM pre-fetched all three
via \texttt{scripts/fetch\_latin.py} (Perseus TEI
\texttt{phi028}/\texttt{phi030}/\texttt{phi029}) and dispatched six
worker sub-agents in one batch --- four splitting \textit{Pro Plancio}
by contiguous section-range (\textsection\textsection 1--26, 27--52,
53--78, 79--104) into part files under a shared register brief
(\texttt{056bc-pro-balbo} as the voice template), and one each
translating \textit{Pro Rabirio Postumo} and \textit{Pro Scauro}
end-to-end. The PM assembled the four \textit{Plancio} parts into
\texttt{english/speeches/054bc-pro-plancio.tex}, wrote that speech's
headnote itself (whole-speech understanding needed), flipped all three
statuses, and ran the consolidation scripts. Per-work parallel sidecars
were generated by \texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (104 / 48 / 76
segments, zero empties) rather than emitted by workers, to remove a
JSON-malformation risk; this is reliable because backfill aligns
mechanically on \texttt{\textbackslash ciceroSection} labels.

\textbf{Fragmentary-speech handling (\textit{Pro Scauro}):} the speech
survives only in fragments (Asconius and the grammarians) plus the
Ambrosian palimpsest, so its section labels are NOT a 1..N run but
editorial sigla: \texttt{a, c, d, e, f, g, h, i, k, m, n, o, p, 1q, 1r,
2s, 2--45, 45a--45n, 46o, 47--51} (\texttt{45e/45i/45j} are absent from
the source too). The English file reproduces these exact labels;
\texttt{validate.py} and the parallel-sidecar alignment confirm an
exact label-set match against the Latin.

\textbf{Headnote correction committed this session:} the \textit{Pro
Rabirio Postumo} headnote initially mis-stated Rabirius's parentage
(``natural son of Cicero's sister''); corrected to the accurate
genealogy --- son of the publicanus C. Curtius, adopted by the will of
his maternal uncle C. Rabirius (no relation to Cicero), as the speech
itself states at \textsection 3. \textbf{No date corrections}: these
are speeches with no Perseus datelines; the conventional 54 BC dating
is retained (\textit{Pro Rabirio Postumo} stays \texttt{year\_range}
-0054 to -0053) and all three files are correctly named \texttt{054bc-}.
\textbf{No Greek phrases} occur in any of the three.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (deferred to a future enrichment
pass to avoid concurrent-write contention):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\textit{Pro Plancio} \S 60} --- the centrepiece
sententia \textit{honorum gradus pares, gloriae dispares} rendered
``the steps of office are equal for the highest men and the lowest; the
steps of glory are unequal.''
\item \textbf{\textit{Pro Plancio} \S\S 36ff} --- \textit{editicii
iudices} rendered ``named jurors''; \textit{lex Licinia de sodaliciis}
glossed parenthetically on first occurrence, then kept Latin;
\textit{sodalitas}/\textit{sodales} = ``sodality''/``club-fellows.''
\item \textbf{\textit{Pro Rabirio Postumo}} --- the \textit{quo ea
pecunia pervenerit} clause rendered consistently ``to whom that money
came''; \textit{dioecetes} and \textit{pallium} kept as loaded
technical terms (glossary candidates).
\item \textbf{\textit{Pro Scauro}} --- damaged/broken fragments
(\texttt{n}; \texttt{51} = the single word \textit{universe}) rendered
as clean fragments with an ellipsis at the broken edge, not padded; the
Sardinian-witness invective (\textit{mastruca}, \textit{pelliti}) kept
literal.
\end{itemize}

session. This is the chronologically-earliest pending non-deferred
work and the first rhetorical treatise drafted since \textit{De
Oratore}. Latin fetched (70{,}214 chars, Perseus TEI
\texttt{phi0474.phi038}, a single self-contained dialogue).

\textbf{Method (parallel-by-section-range, new this session):} the PM
dispatched five worker sub-agents in parallel, each translating a
contiguous 28-section range (\textsection\textsection 1--28, 29--56,
57--84, 85--112, 113--140) into a separate part file under a single
shared locked-conventions brief (speaker tags, a fixed technical
glossary, and the STYLE.md voice); the PM then assembled the five
parts into the one \texttt{english/rhetoric/054bc-partitiones-oratoriae.tex},
wrote the three-paragraph headnote, flipped the status, and ran the
consolidation scripts. The catechism's dense stichomythic Q\&A is
front-loaded (\textsection\textsection 1--16, $\sim$66 speaker turns)
and tapers to near-continuous exposition by \textsection 85. Speaker
convention: the Latin labels the questioning son by the family name
(\texttt{C.}/\texttt{Cicero.}) against the answering father
(\texttt{P.}/\texttt{Pater.}); rendered \texttt{\textbackslash textsc\{Marcus.\}}
(son) and \texttt{\textbackslash textsc\{Cicero.\}} (father) for
clarity, with the choice explained in the headnote.

\textbf{Latin metadata correction committed during session 50:} the
Perseus TEI fetch misplaced the \textsection 86 milestone as a bare
inline ``86'' inside \textsection 85's text (``\ldots Qua re quoniam 86
in his versatur\ldots''). Restored a proper
\texttt{\textbackslash ciceroSection\{86\}} at the natural sentence
break (\textsection 86 begins ``Qua re quoniam in his versatur omne
consilium duobus\ldots''). The Latin file now carries all 140
contiguous sections (1--140, no gaps or dups), and the parallel
sidecar aligns 140 segments with no empties. \textbf{No date
correction}: the work's date is conventional and disputed (c. 54--46
BC); the meta entry's year-precision \texttt{-0054-07-01} is retained
and the file is correctly named \texttt{054bc-}. \textbf{No Greek
phrases} occur in the work; \texttt{greek-phrases.json} is unaffected.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the shared
jsonl directly, to avoid concurrent-write contention; deferred to a
future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\textit{propositum} (\S\S 61--68)} rendered ``thesis''
(the abstract kernel of the indefinite question), a fresh choice
alongside the locked ``indefinite question / general inquiry.''
\item \textbf{\textit{expressa} (\S 6, \textit{oratio aut libera aut
expressa})} rendered ``extracted under pressure'' (testimony squeezed
out under duress), to avoid collision with \textit{elocutio} =
``expression.''
\item \textbf{\textit{praevaricatio} / \textit{praevaricator}
(\S\S 124--126)} rendered ``collusion'' / ``collusive defender,'' with
the \textit{varus} etymology kept literal.
\item \textbf{\textit{penus} / \textit{ruta caesa} (\S 107)} rendered
``the store'' / ``the fixtures and fittings'' (legal terms of art
quoted as definitional examples; candidates for a glossary note).
\item \textbf{\textit{ratio} / \textit{firmamentum} (\S 103)} rendered
``ground'' / ``buttress'' (a paired forensic opposition).
\item \textbf{\S 118} ``the conspiracy carried out in my consulship'' =
the Catilinarian conspiracy of 63 BC; kept literal per the
no-bracketed-notes rule, flagged for a possible glossary/crossref note.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Validation:} \texttt{validate.py} clean --- 8 warnings = the
pre-existing orphan-Latin (\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49}) plus the
chronological-gap warning, now at \textbf{32} pending earlier-dated
works (down from 33 post-session-49, the expected $-1$ decrement, since
\textit{Partitiones} at 54 BC fell inside the chronological-gap zone).
\textbf{Latest drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad
Familiares} 10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains the marker.

\textbf{Suggested next translation batch} (when session 50 is landed
via \texttt{bash scripts/cowork\_handoff.sh "session 50: \ldots"} and
session 51 opens):

\textbf{Continue the Phase 3 major-works sweep in chronological
order.} The chronologically-next pending non-deferred works after

\textit{pro-scauro} (pro-scauro survives only in fragments), then

\textit{tusculanae-disputationes}, \textit{cato-maior-de-senectute},
\textit{de-divinatione}, \textit{de-fato}, \textit{topica},
\textit{laelius-de-amicitia}, \textit{de-officiis}, and the 43 BC

**Project management is now Claude Cowork** (running on Alexander's

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-49):} \textbf{919 /
958 works drafted (\~95.9\%)} --- session 49 completed
\textit{Philippic 14} (21 April 43 BC, 38 sections, $\sim$5{,}400
English words) end-to-end in one session, in single-substantial-
speech mode. \textbf{This is the last of the Philippics and the last
surviving speech of Cicero; drafting it completes the Philippics
(1--14 all drafted).} Latin fetched (24{,}980 chars, Perseus TEI
\texttt{phi0474.phi035} selecting \texttt{speech\_index: '14'}, a
single speech and not a whole-book dump). Drafted in eight Edit
chunks (\textsection 1--5, 6--10, 11--15, 16--20, 21--25, 26--30,
31--35, 36--38). Three-paragraph headnote written (the speech answers
a motion to resume the toga after the victory at Forum Gallorum;
Cicero resists premature celebration since the war ends only with the
relief of Decimus Brutus at Mutina, then turns the supplicatio into an
implicit declaration of Antony as \textit{hostis} by moving an
unprecedented fifty-day thanksgiving and naming Hirtius, Pansa, and
the young Caesar \textit{imperatores}; \textsection 14--16 rebut the
rumour that Cicero meant to seize the fasces on the Parilia; the
peroration, \textsection 31--38, is the funeral oration for the fallen
of the Martian and Fourth legions and the formal motion for a
monument and rewards). Status flipped \texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} regenerated the parallel sidecar
(38 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-14.json}, $\sim$58KB,
verified section-aligned 1--38);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} (958 entries) and \texttt{validate.py}
both run clean (8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at \textbf{33} pending earlier-dated works, down from 34
post-session-48, the expected $-1$ decrement). \textbf{Latest
drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares}
10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains the marker, since \textit{Philippic
14} is dated 21 April 43 BC and so falls inside the chronological-gap
zone.

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 49.} The
meta entry's day-precision date (\texttt{-0043-04-21}) is the
conventional date for the speech and is correct; the Philippics carry
no Perseus dateline. Perseus TEI numbering for Philippic 14 is regular
(no anomaly), and the fetch returned the single speech cleanly. The
\texttt{043bc-philippic-14} files are correctly named; no new rename
carry-overs in session 49.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 1--3 \textit{saga}/\textit{toga}.} The
war-cloak/civilian-dress antithesis drives the exordium: Rome had put
on the \textit{sagum} for the peril of Decimus Brutus, and the toga is
to be reserved for the completed victory. Rendered ``war-cloak'' for
\textit{sagum} and ``toga''/``civilian dress'' for
\textit{togati}/\textit{vestitus}, keeping the running opposition
without a footnote.
\item \textbf{\S 6 \textit{imbuti}\ldots\textit{madefacti}.} The
intensification kept: the swords ``have been dipped --- or rather
drenched.''
\item \textbf{\S 6 \textit{quo usque}\ldots\textit{hostis}.} The
deliberate echo of the Catilinarian \textit{quo usque tandem};
rendered naturally (``How long, then, shall the man who has outdone
all enemies in wickedness go without the name of enemy?'') without
flagging the allusion in the body.
\item \textbf{\S 14 fasces / Parilia.} The rumour that Cicero would
descend to the Forum with the fasces on the Parilia (21 April --- the
speech's own day) and make himself tyrant; the augural questions
(``By what auspices\ldots how long\ldots to whom hand them on?'')
preserved as a tricolon.
\item \textbf{\S 23 supplicatio precedents.} The Sulla/Cinna/Caesar
examples that no thanksgiving was ever decreed for victory in a civil
war drive the central argument: to decree one now is already to judge
Antony's men \textit{hostes}. ``Decreed, do I say? It was not even
asked for in the victor's dispatches.''
\item \textbf{\S 27 \textit{O solem ipsum beatissimum}.} The
apostrophe to the Sun who, before he set, saw Antony fleeing with his
few --- kept as direct apostrophe.
\item \textbf{\S 31 \textit{O fortunata mors}.} The funeral-oration
tag kept: ``O fortunate death, which, owed to nature, was paid above
all for the fatherland.''
\item \textbf{\S 32 Mars / Martia.} The etymological play preserved
--- the legion ``whose very name is from Mars,'' and ``Mars himself is
wont to claim as his own pledge the bravest from the battle-line.''
\item \textbf{\S 34 \textit{ara virtutis} (long period).} The
suspended consolation period rendered with its build intact to the
landing, ``an altar of valour\ldots unto the memory of eternity.''
\item \textbf{\S 36--38 the formal \textit{sententia}.} Decree
language rendered in a ``Whereas\ldots the Senate resolves
that\ldots'' register; \textit{alter ambove} = ``the one or both.''
\item \textbf{No Greek phrases} occur in this speech;
\texttt{greek-phrases.json} is unaffected by this work.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Suggested next translation batch} (when session 49 is landed
via \texttt{bash scripts/cowork\_handoff.sh "session 49: \ldots"} and
session 50 opens):

snapshot had flagged it as a \emph{likely} two-session split, but it
was completed cleanly in a single session in ten Edit chunks
(\textsection 1--5, 6--10, 11--15, 16--20, 21--25, 26--30, 31--35,
36--40, 41--45, 46--50). Latin fetched (37{,}613 chars, Perseus TEI
\texttt{phi0474.phi035} selecting \texttt{speech\_index: '13'}, a
single speech and not a whole-book dump). Three-paragraph headnote
written (the speech answers a fresh peace overture --- M.~Aemilius
Lepidus's dispatch urging concord --- and turns Lepidus's own
authority against the peace he counsels; its centrepiece, \textsection
22--48, is the clause-by-clause reading and demolition of a letter
Antony had written to Hirtius and the young Caesar, forwarded to
Cicero by Hirtius; the first movement, \textsection 1--21, argues no
peace is possible with the Antonian faction and presses the case for
restoring the younger Magnus Pompeius; the peroration, \textsection
49--50, refuses all reconciliation and moves a decree commending
Pompeius). Status flipped \texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} regenerated the parallel sidecar
(50 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-13.json}, $\sim$89KB,
verified section-aligned 1--50);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} (958 entries) and \texttt{validate.py}
both run clean (8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at \textbf{34} pending earlier-dated works, down from 35
post-session-47, the expected $-1$ decrement). \textbf{Latest
drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares}
10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains the marker, since \textit{Philippic
13} is dated March 43 BC and so falls inside the chronological-gap
zone.

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 48.} The
meta entry's day-precision date (\texttt{-0043-03-20}) is the
conventional date for the speech and is correct; the Philippics carry
no Perseus dateline. Perseus TEI numbering for Philippic 13 is regular
(no anomaly analogous to the Fam.\ 13.49 case), and the fetch returned
the single speech cleanly. The \texttt{043bc-philippic-13} files are
correctly named; no new rename carry-overs in session 48.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 17 \textit{maestitiam atque lacrimas}.} The
antecedent of \textit{eius} (and the subject of \textit{abiectus,
confectus esset}) is Antony, named in the \textit{cum}-clause: the
dejection and tears at the Lupercalia are Antony's own self-abasement
in offering Caesar the diadem (``would rather be that man's slave than
his colleague''), not the people's grief. Rendered ``his dejection and
his tears.''
\item \textbf{\S 22 \textit{O Spartace}.} Antony apostrophised as
``Spartacus''; the gladiator-slave insult, with Catiline made to seem
bearable by comparison.
\item \textbf{\S 24 \textit{mentum mentemque}.} The chin/mind pun
kept: ``you would lay your chin --- and your mind --- in the laps of
actresses.''
\item \textbf{\S 25 \textit{venefica}.} The feminine ``poisoner-woman''
hurled at Decimus Brutus; the \textit{venefica}/\textit{veneficia}
wordplay preserved (``do you dare call `poisoner' the man who has found
the remedies against your poisons?''). Also \textit{nove Hannibal} ---
``you new Hannibal.''
\item \textbf{\S 27 \textit{Muribus Deciis}.} The pun on the Decii
Mures (``the Mice'') and \textit{arrosit} (``gnawed at Caesar's
gifts'') --- mouse-imagery --- preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 35 \textit{parentasset} (crux).} A dense, compressed
counterfactual: ``Dolabella would have paid the death-offering to his
commander no sooner than Antony would have paid his to his colleague
as well.'' Rendered literally and flagged; the surrounding sentence is
heavily ironic.
\item \textbf{\S 40 \textit{lanista}.} ``Trainer of gladiators'';
\textit{unius corporis duas acies} = ``two battle-lines of one body''
(the body politic). Cicero accepts the label: ``I want the worse
slaughtered and the better to win.''
\item \textbf{\S 43 \textit{piissimus}.} The famous grammatical jab
rendered with its point intact and no footnote (per STYLE.md): a word
``that does not exist at all in the Latin tongue,'' which Antony, for
his ``divine piety,'' coins afresh.
\item \textbf{\S 49 \textit{prius undis flamma} / \textit{nec facta nec
nomen}.} Two poetic tags (``as some poet or other says'') kept in
quotation marks with the literal adynaton image (``sooner shall fire
mingle with the waves'').
\item \textbf{No Greek phrases} occur in this speech;
\texttt{greek-phrases.json} is unaffected by this work.
\end{itemize}

via \texttt{bash scripts/cowork\_handoff.sh "session 48: \ldots"} and
session 49 opens):

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-47):} \textbf{917 /
958 works drafted (\~95.7\%)} --- session 47 completed
\textit{Philippic 12} (March 43 BC, 30 sections, $\sim$5{,}100
English words) end-to-end in one session, in single-substantial-
speech mode. Latin fetched (21{,}967 chars, Perseus TEI
\texttt{phi0474.phi035} selecting \texttt{speech\_index: '12'}, a
single speech and not a whole-book dump), English translated in six
Edit chunks (\textsection 1--5, 6--10, 11--15, 16--20, 21--25,
26--30), three-paragraph headnote written (the speech of retraction:
Cicero withdraws both his assent to, and his own person from, the
proposed second peace embassy to Antony at Mutina; the two consulars
L.~Calpurnius Piso and Q.~Fufius Calenus, intimates of Antony, with
the consul Pansa lending weight, had revived the hope of an
``honourable peace'' and Cicero had let himself be named to the
mission; the speech argues that the embassy is folly and demoralizing
to towns, colonies, Italy, and the legions (the Martian and the
Fourth), that no peace is possible without unmaking the Senate's own
decrees --- the forged minutes, the laws carried by violence, the
seven hundred million sesterces embezzled, the offices and kingdoms
sold --- and dwells on Cicero's personal danger on the three roads to
Mutina (Flaminia, Aurelia, Cassia), closing conditionally: his life
to be kept for the commonwealth, the question measured by the public
good, and he will go only if it can be done safely). Status flipped
\texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} regenerated the parallel sidecar
(30 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-12.json}, $\sim$51KB);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} (958 entries) and \texttt{validate.py}
both run clean (8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at 35 pending earlier-dated works, down from 36
post-session-46, the expected $-1$ decrement). \textbf{Latest
drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares}
10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains the marker, since \textit{Philippic
12} is dated March 43 BC and so falls inside the chronological-gap
zone.

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 47.} The
meta entry's month-precision date (\texttt{-0043-03-01}) was left as
is: the Philippics carry no Perseus dateline, and the conventional

for Philippic 12 is regular (no anomaly analogous to the Fam.\ 13.49
case), and the fetch returned the single speech cleanly (21{,}967
chars, not a whole-book dump). The \texttt{043bc-philippic-12} files
are correctly named; no new rename carry-overs in session 47.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 3, \S 5 \textit{caligo}.} The ``mist'' that ``had
dazzled the edge of my mind'' (\textit{praestrinxerat aciem animi},
\S 3) and is then ``scattered'' (\textit{discussa est illa caligo},
\S 5): the optical metaphor kept consistent across both sections.
\item \textbf{\S 5 \textit{cuiusvis hominis est errare; nullius nisi
insipientis perseverare in errore}.} Rendered fresh (``Any man may
err; none but a fool persists in his error'') rather than echoing the
proverbial ``to err is human.''
\item \textbf{\S 7 \textit{optimus est portus paenitenti mutatio
consili}.} ``The best harbour for a man who repents is a change of
plan'' --- the harbour image kept.
\item \textbf{\S 12 \textit{figentur} \ldots\ \textit{refixistis}.}
The bronze-tablet pun (the posted decrees fixed up / taken down):
``shall those tablets be fixed up again which you by your decrees took
down.''
\item \textbf{\S 12 \textit{eluatur} \ldots\ \textit{elui}.} Blood
``washed away'' vs.\ the renown of cruelty ``washed out'' --- the
wash pun preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 14 \textit{Tirones Numisios et mustelas Seios}.}
Contemptuous plural naming two of Antony's henchmen (Tiro and
Mustela); rendered ``such creatures as Numisius Tiro and Mustela
Seius.'' \textit{mustela} = ``weasel''; a possible pun on the cognomen
is flagged for the enrichment pass.
\item \textbf{\S 17 \textit{hostem} / \textit{adversarium};
\textit{bellum} / \textit{tumultum}.} The antitheses preserved (``an
enemy, when others called him an adversary; \ldots\ a war, when others
called it a tumult'').
\item \textbf{\S 20 \textit{ne aquilam quidem ipsam}.} The pun on
Aquila (the centurion's name) and \textit{aquila} (the legionary
eagle): ``not even the Eagle herself would have believed him.''
\item \textbf{\S 26 \textit{ne vino quidem permixta temperari}.} The
wine-tempering pun --- Antony's savagery not ``tempered'' even by an
admixture of wine --- kept on ``temper.''
\item \textbf{\S 27 \textit{voluntate hospitem, necessitate
hostem}.} Scato's reply, with the \textit{hospitem}/\textit{hostem}
near-pun: ``By goodwill a guest, by necessity a foe.''
\item \textbf{No Greek phrases} occur in this speech;
\texttt{greek-phrases.json} is unaffected by this work.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Suggested next translation batch} (when session 47 is landed
via \texttt{bash scripts/cowork\_handoff.sh "session 47: \ldots"} and
session 48 opens):

\textbf{Session 48 opens \textit{Philippic 13} (20 March 43 BC,
$\sim$50 sections).} The longest of the remaining Philippics: Cicero
reads out Antony's letter to Hirtius and the young Caesar and answers
it clause by clause, and rejects any accommodation with the
Antonians. \textbf{Likely a two-session split} (e.g.\ \textsection
1--25 then \textsection 26--50), per the long-speech discipline in
\texttt{CLAUDE.md} (\textsection Timeout discipline: $\sim$5 sections
per Edit, WIP between chunks). The meta date is day-precision
(\texttt{-0043-03-20}); no correction expected.

\textbf{After \textit{Philippic 13}}: \textit{Philippic 14}
(21 April 43 BC, $\sim$38 sections, the last of the series, on the
victory at Mutina and honours for the fallen). Philippic phase after
this session: \textbf{2 speeches left (Phil 13--14; Phil 1--12 now
completed).}

\textbf{After the Philippics} (sessions $\sim$50--62 or so): sweep
the major philosophical treatises and the remaining minor speeches

(29{,}335 chars, Perseus TEI \texttt{phi0474.phi035} selecting

command the war; Cicero's rejection of the two standing motions ---
L.~Caesar's extraordinary command for the \textit{privatus}
P.~Servilius Isauricus, and the assignment of Asia and Syria to the
consuls Pansa and Hirtius while D.~Brutus is besieged at Mutina; the
natural-law defence of Cassius's self-authorization
(\textsection 28, \textit{lex nihil aliud nisi recta ratio}); and
Cicero's own formal \textit{senatus consultum} (\textsection 29--31)
confirming C.~Cassius in Syria \textit{optimo iure}, taking over the
armies of Q.~Marcius Crispus, L.~Staius Murcus, and A.~Allienus,
with the kings Deiotarus father and son and the other client kings
invited to aid him). Status flipped
\texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} regenerated the parallel sidecar
(40 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-11.json}, $\sim$69KB);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} and \texttt{validate.py} both run clean
(8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at 36 pending earlier-dated works, down from 37
post-session-45, the expected $-1$ decrement). \textbf{Latest
drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares}
10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains the marker, since \textit{Philippic
11} is dated March 43 BC and so falls inside the chronological-gap

meta entry's month-precision date (\texttt{-0043-03-01}) was left as
is: the Philippics carry no Perseus dateline, and the conventional
placing is only ``late February / March 43 BC,'' so day-precision is

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 14 \textit{novarum tabularum novam tabulam}.} Pun
on \textit{novae tabulae} (cancellation of debts) and \textit{tabula}
(an auction / bankruptcy posting): Trebellius, champion of
debt-cancellation, ``turned into a new account-book himself.''
\item \textbf{\S 14 \textit{Lysidicum ipsum \ldots\ iure Germanum
Cimber occidit}.} Double pun: Annius Cimber's father was named
Lysidicus (Greek ``loosener of suits''), so Cicero calls Cimber
``Lysidicus himself'' for dissolving every law; \textit{Germanum} =
both ``his blood brother'' and ``a German,'' and the cognomen Cimber
names a Germanic tribe, so a Cimbrian killing a German is darkly
``by right.'' Rendered ``unless perhaps it was by right that the
Cimbrian killed his own brother, a German.''
\item \textbf{\S 17 \textit{populare atque ventosum}.} Of
extraordinary commands; rendered ``a popular, windy thing,'' keeping
the wind image rather than flattening to ``fickle.''
\item \textbf{\S 18 \textit{pro consulibus \ldots\ non pro
consule}.} The L.~Philippus pun: \textit{pro consulibus} (in place
of the two consuls, who refused) vs.\ \textit{pro consule} (as
proconsul). Rendered ``in place of the consuls --- not as a
proconsul.''
\item \textbf{\S 24 \textit{id signum quod de caelo delapsum Vestae
custodiis continetur}.} The Palladium (image of Pallas fallen from
heaven, kept in Vesta's temple); rendered descriptively, the proper
name kept out of the body per the no-body-notes rule.
\item \textbf{\S 26 \textit{solet ipse accipere manicas}.} Of
C.~Antonius; rendered ``he is wont to hold out his own wrists for the
manacles'' --- the jibe that he surrenders rather than withstand a
siege.
\item \textbf{\S 28 \textit{lex nihil aliud nisi recta et a numine
deorum tracta ratio}.} The natural-law definition, rendered freshly
(``law is nothing else than right reason, drawn from the will of the
gods, commanding what is honourable and forbidding its opposite'')
rather than echoing the familiar \textit{De Re Publica} 3 /
Lactantius phrasings.
\item \textbf{\S 35 \textit{maximam eius et singularem laudem
praetermitto}.} The \textit{praeteritio} of Cassius's part in
Caesar's assassination (``not yet welcome to all''), preserved as
such.
\item \textbf{\S 5, \S 7 \textit{Samiarius}.} The executioner to
whom Trebonius was handed: rendered ``a Samian exile'' (\S 5) and
``the Samian torturer and executioner'' (\S 7), kept consistent. The
word may instead mean ``armourer / sword-polisher''; flagged for the
enrichment pass.
\item \textbf{No Greek phrases} occur in this speech;
\texttt{greek-phrases.json} is unaffected by this work.
\end{itemize}

\textit{Philippic 10} (February 43 BC, 26 sections,
$\sim$4{,}316 English words) end-to-end in one session: Latin
fetched (19{,}096 chars, Perseus TEI \texttt{phi0474.phi035}
selecting \texttt{speech\_index: '10'}, a single speech and not a
whole-book dump), English translated in single-substantial-speech
mode in five Edit chunks (\textsection 1--5, 6--10, 11--15,
16--20, 21--26), three-paragraph headnote written (the speech of
confirmation seconding the consul Pansa and answering Q.\ Fufius
Calenus, delivered after M. Brutus's despatch reported that he had
brought Macedonia, Illyricum, and all Greece into the power of the
Senate and Roman people; the governing principle that all legions
belong to the commonwealth and that a man forfeits army and command
the moment he turns them on the state; the answer to the
veterans-distrust objection by widening the ground beyond the
veterans alone; the death-before-slavery creed of
\textsection 19--20 (\textit{non in spiritu vita est, sed ea nulla
est omnino servienti}); the muster of Brutus's lieutenants ---
Q.~Hortensius, the younger Cicero, Cn.~Domitius, M.~Apuleius, P.~Vatinius; and the
closing \textit{senatus consultum} of \textsection 25--26
confirming Brutus, styled by his adoptive name \textit{Q.\ Caepio
Brutus pro consule}, in command of Macedonia, Illyricum, and Greece
with power to raise money, contract loans, requisition grain, and
keep his forces near Italy, and continuing Hortensius in the
province until a successor is named). Status flipped
\texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} regenerated the parallel sidecar
(26 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-10.json}, $\sim$45KB);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} and \texttt{validate.py} both run clean
(8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at 37 pending earlier-dated works, down from 38

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 45.} The
meta entry's month-precision date (\texttt{-0043-02-01}) was left
as is: the Philippics, unlike the letters, carry no Perseus
dateline, and the conventional placing is only ``February 43 BC,''

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 5 \textit{di meliora!}} rendered ``The gods
forbid!'' --- keeping the Roman plural gods (per STYLE.md's
no-Christianizing rule) rather than ``God forbid!''; it is Calenus's
imagined reply when asked whether he would wish his son like Brutus
or like an Antonius.
\item \textbf{\S 5--6 the clerk jibe.} \textit{librarium Bruti
laudare, non Brutum} rendered ``to praise Brutus's clerk, and not
Brutus''; Calenus's praise of the letter as \textit{recte et ordine
scriptas} quoted as ``well and properly written,'' tied to the
\S 6 mockery that the Senate has never decreed a letter ``well
written.''
\item \textbf{\S 11 \textit{caducae hereditates}} rendered
``windfall inheritances'' (the lapsed estates C.~Antonius stopped to
plunder on the road), preserving the joke that otherwise ``you would
say he had flown, not made a journey.''
\item \textbf{\S 17--18 section split at \textit{quae, malum!}} The
Latin numbering breaks the sentence \textit{quae, malum, est ista
ratio\ldots} across the section boundary; the English break is
placed inside it too (\S 17 ends ``What, confound it ---'', \S 18
begins ``is this the method\ldots''), preserving the Latin grain.
\textit{malum} as interjection rendered ``confound it.''
\item \textbf{\S 19--20 the death-before-slavery creed.}
\textit{non enim in spiritu vita est, sed ea nulla est omnino
servienti} rendered ``life does not consist in breath; rather,
there is none at all for one who is a slave''; \textit{Martem
communem} rendered ``Mars belongs to both sides alike'' (Roman form
kept, not flattened to ``the chance of war'').
\item \textbf{\S 24--25 and \S 25--26 section splits across names.}
As in the Philippic 9 treatment, the breaks fall inside the names
--- \S 24 ends ``on the matter that Gaius''~\textbar~\S 25 ``Pansa
the consul\ldots'', and \S 25 ends ``and that Quintus''~\textbar~%
\S 26 ``Caepio Brutus, proconsul\ldots'' --- preserving the Latin
section grain inside the quoted \textit{senatus consultum}.
\item \textbf{\S 22 \textit{cum tabulis novis redit in gratiam}}
rendered ``now, with a cancellation of debts, comes back into
favour''; \textit{Saxae et Cafones} (contemptuous plurals of
Antony's henchmen Saxa and Cafo) rendered ``the Saxas and the
Cafos.''
\item \textbf{No Greek phrases} occur in this speech;
\texttt{greek-phrases.json} is unaffected by this work.
\end{itemize}

## Where to resume now (session 44 legacy snapshot below)

**Project management is now Claude Cowork** (running on Alexander's
desktop, on his Max subscription quota). When Cowork opens this
repo, this is the first section to read; \texttt{PLAN.md} is the
binding multi-surface roadmap.

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-44):} \textbf{914 /
958 works drafted (\~95.4\%)} --- session 44 completed
\textit{Philippic 9} (4 February 43 BC, 17 sections,
$\sim$2{,}639 English words) end-to-end in one session: Latin
fetched (11{,}721 chars, Perseus TEI \texttt{phi0474.phi035}
selecting \texttt{speech\_index: '9'}, a single speech and not a
whole-book dump), English translated in single-substantial-speech
mode in four Edit chunks (\textsection 1--5, 6--10, 11--15,
16--17), three-paragraph headnote written (the funeral
\textit{laudatio} for Servius Sulpicius Rufus --- consul 51 BC and
the foremost jurist of the age, who died on the embassy to Antony
at Mutina; the speech answers P. Servilius Vatia Isauricus, who
would grant a statue only to an envoy killed by the sword, with the
argument that the ancestors looked to the \textit{cause} of death
and not its \textit{manner}; the \textit{exempla} of the four
envoys slain by Lars Tolumnius at Fidenae and of Cn. Octavius
murdered at Laodicea, both given statues on the Rostra; the
connoisseur's choice of a bronze \textit{pedestrian} statue over a
gilded equestrian one, in keeping with Sulpicius's love of
old-Roman restraint; and the closing \textit{senatus consultum} of
\textsection 15--17 --- statue, reserved family space at the games,
inscription, public funeral, and a public tomb in the Esquiline
field). Status flipped \texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} regenerated the parallel sidecar
(17 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-9.json}, $\sim$27KB);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} and \texttt{validate.py} both run clean
(8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at 38 pending earlier-dated works, down from 39
post-session-43, the expected $-1$ decrement). \textbf{Latest
drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares}
10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains the marker, since \textit{Philippic
9} is dated 4 February 43 BC and so falls inside the
chronological-gap zone.

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 44.} The
meta entry was already day-precision and correct; Perseus TEI
numbering for Philippic 9 is regular (no anomaly analogous to the
Fam.\ 13.49 case), and the fetch returned the single speech cleanly
(11{,}721 chars, not a whole-book dump).

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 3 the legal pivot.} \textit{causam mortis
censuerint, non genus esse quaerendum} rendered ``they held the
cause of death, not its manner, was the thing to be looked to'' ---
\textit{genus} as ``manner'' rather than ``kind/type,'' since the
antithesis is cause-versus-mode-of-death, the hinge of the whole
argument against P. Servilius.
\item \textbf{\S 13 the statue.} \textit{aenea statua \ldots et ea
pedestris quam inaurata equestris} rendered ``a bronze statue, and
that one on foot \ldots than a gilded equestrian one'';
\textit{pedestris} kept as ``on foot''/``pedestrian'' to preserve
the point about old-Roman restraint, with the Latin word surfaced
once in the headnote. \textit{tamquam ex eius auctoritate et
voluntate} $\to$ ``as though by his own authority and will.''
\item \textbf{\S 13--14 section split across a name.} The Latin
numbering breaks \textit{P.}~\textbar~\textit{Servili} mid-name;
the English section break is placed so it also falls inside the
name (``the proposal of Publius''~\textbar~``Servilius''),
preserving the Latin grain rather than smoothing it over.
\item \textbf{\S 15--16 section split inside the decree.} Same
treatment: the break falls across \textit{Ser.}~\textbar~%
\textit{Sulpicius} (``in which Servius''~\textbar~``Sulpicius was
often of great service'') inside the quoted \textit{senatus
consultum}.
\item \textbf{\S 15--17 the \textit{senatus consultum}.} The decree
rendered inside `` '' quotation marks; the tribal formula
\textit{Q. f. lemonia Rufus} rendered ``son of Quintus, of the
Lemonian tribe, Rufus''; \textit{uti quod optimo iure publice
sepulcrum datum esset} $\to$ ``under the same right as a tomb
granted at public expense under the best title.''
\item \textbf{No Greek phrases} occur in this speech;
\texttt{greek-phrases.json} is unaffected by this work.
\end{itemize}

Senate had decreed a \textit{tumultus} and the war-cloaks on
Lucius Caesar's milder motion but balked at the word
\textit{bellum} --- and the speech's structure: the lexical
argument that there can be war without tumult but no tumult
without war (\textsection 1--4), the proof that this is a war
whatever it is called (\textsection 5--8), the antithesis of
Antony's promises and the Senate's (\textsection 9--10), the
named duel with Q. Fufius Calenus over whether slavery can be
called peace (\textsection 11--19, with the \textit{exempla} of
justified senatorial force --- Nasica, Opimius, Marius, Catiline
--- and the surgeon's metaphor of the amputated limb), the
indictment of the second embassy and Antony's ``modest''
counter-demands (\textsection 20--28, with the Popilius Laenas
line-in-the-sand \textit{exemplum}), the digression on the duty
of a princeps and the example of the aged augur Scaevola
(\textsection 29--32), and the formal motion of
amnesty-before-the-Ides-of-March (\textsection 33)). Status
flipped \texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} regenerated the parallel sidecar
(33 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-8.json},

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 2--4 the \textit{bellum}/\textit{tumultus}
distinction.} \textit{potest enim esse bellum ut tumultus non
sit, tumultus autem esse sine bello non potest} rendered ``there
can be a war such that there is no tumult; but a tumult cannot
exist without a war.'' \textit{Tumultus} kept as the cognate
``tumult'' throughout, since the whole argument turns on the word
itself; the folk-etymology behind \textit{unde etiam nomen ductum
est} (from \textit{timor multus}) left implicit (``from which the
very name `tumult' is derived''), per STYLE.md's bar on
footnoting in the body.
\item \textbf{\S 5 \textit{Gallia vastatur: quae pax potest esse
certior?}} Rendered with an exclamation --- ``Gaul is being laid
waste: what peace could be more secure!'' --- rather than a
question, so the sarcasm lands in English.
\item \textbf{\S 9 \textit{di meliora!}} Rendered ``May the gods
grant us better!'', not ``God forbid,'' per STYLE.md's
no-Christianizing rule.
\item \textbf{\S 9 \textit{hasta Caesaris}.} Rendered ``Caesar's
auction-spear'' (the spear set up to mark a public auction of
confiscated property), keeping the Roman image; the recurring
\textit{hastam videre cupiunt} rendered ``longing to see the
spear.''
\item \textbf{\S 23 \textit{virgula stantem circumscripsit}.} The
Popilius Laenas line-in-the-sand: ``with a little rod drew a
circle around him as he stood''; \textit{senatus faciem secum
attulerat} $\to$ ``he had brought with him the very face of the
Senate.''
\item \textbf{\S 26--27 the demands catalogue.} \textit{rationes
ad Opis} / \textit{septiens miliens} rendered ``the accounts at
the temple of Ops'' / ``the seven hundred million'';
\textit{vii viris} kept as ``the Septemviri''; \textit{Galliam
togatam $\ldots$ comatam} rendered ``the toga-clad Gaul $\ldots$
the long-haired,'' preserving the Cisalpine/Transalpine wordplay.
\item \textbf{\S 32 \textit{cum est in sagis civitas}.} The
consular's privilege of keeping the toga during a
\textit{tumultus} rendered ``even when the state is in
war-cloaks''; Cicero announces he waives it in solidarity.
\end{itemize}

## Where to resume now (session 42 legacy snapshot below)

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-42):} \textbf{912 /
958 works drafted (\~95.2\%)} --- session 42 completed
\textit{Philippic 7} (mid-January 43 BC, 27 sections,
$\sim$4{,}050 English words) end-to-end in one session: Latin
fetched (16{,}031 chars, Perseus TEI \texttt{phi0474.phi035}
selecting \texttt{speech\_index: '7'}), English translated in
single-substantial-speech mode in six Edit chunks
(\textsection 1--5, 6--10, 11--15, 16--20, 21--25, 26--27),
four-paragraph headnote written (composition history --- the
holding speech of the legation interval, delivered in the
Senate in mid-January 43 BC while the legates Sulpicius Rufus,
Piso, and Philippus were still on the road to Antony at
Mutina, called on small business about the Appian Way and the
Mint and the Lupercalia but used to keep the will to war alive
--- and the speech's three-part architecture: \S 1--7 the
proem and encomium of Pansa, \S 8 the tripartite
\textit{propositio} (\textit{pacem cum M. Antonio esse nolo}
because it is \textit{turpis}, \textit{periculosa}, and
\textit{esse non potest}), \S 9--15 the demonstration of
shamefulness (the five-fold anaphora of \textit{non hostem
iudicastis}), \S 16--20 of danger (the Lucius-Antonius
patronage catalogue, redeploying Philippic 6), \S 21--25 of
impossibility (the cascade of constituencies with whom no
peace can hold), \S 26--27 the peroration with the
conditions Antony would have to meet and the warning to
Pansa at the tiller). Status flipped
\texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} regenerated the parallel
sidecar (27 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-7.json}, 37KB);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} and \texttt{validate.py} both run
clean (8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at 40 pending earlier-dated works, down from 41
post-session-41, the expected $-1$ decrement). \textbf{Latest
drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares}
10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains the marker, since
\textit{Philippic 7} is dated mid-January 43 BC and so falls
inside the chronological-gap zone.

\textbf{Cowork session 42 --- 1 substantial speech completed
(PM-direct, 27 sections):}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{PM-direct (\textit{Philippic 7}, mid-January 43
BC, the senatorial holding speech of the legation interval ---
delivered while the legates Sulpicius Rufus, Piso, and
Philippus were on the road to Antony at Mutina, before any
report had come back, prepares the Senate for the inevitable
refusal):} 27 sections, $\sim$4{,}050 English words. A clean
tripartite \textit{sententia}: peace with Antony is
\textit{turpis}, \textit{periculosa}, \textit{esse non
potest} (\S 8, the \textit{propositio}). The first part
(\S 9--15) is shamefulness: the great five-fold anaphora of
\textit{non hostem iudicastis} (\S 10--13) running through
the chain of Senate decrees by which Antony has effectively
been judged an enemy --- the honours to Octavian, the praise
of the veterans and of the legions who deserted Antony, the
elevation of Brutus and the Gallic province, the order that
the consuls go to war, the universal levy --- and the closing
\textit{moriamur} (\S 14) as the rhetorical apex: if we cannot
wrench these arms away, let us die. The middle (\S 16--20)
is danger: the Antonii in Rome, Lucius the murmillo
\textit{patronus} (now compressed into a four-fold
\textit{patronus} anaphora, the statue-by-statue unfolding of
Philippic 6 condensed), the Mylasa murmillo who cut down his
Thracian-armed friend and took a \textit{luculenta plaga} in
return, the warning that if we slip in counsel now, the
wicked will seem to be too many soon. The close (\S 21--25)
is impossibility: a cascade of constituencies with whom no
peace can hold --- Senate, equestrians, the populus Romanus
in the contio, the municipalities, the named exemplum of
Lucius Visidius the Roman equestrian, Caesar Octavianus and
Decimus Brutus, the province of Gaul itself, all
\textit{plena odiorum, plena discordiarum}. The peroration
(\S 26--27) sets out the conditions Antony would have to meet
(cease attacking Brutus, allow legates to approach him, lead
the army across the Rubicon, come within 200 miles of Rome
but no nearer, submit to Senate and Roman people) and closes
with the brilliant reversal: \textit{non illi senatus, sed
ille populo Romano bellum indixerit} --- not the Senate
declaring war on him, but he on the Roman people --- and the
warning to Pansa at the tiller of the ship of state.
\textbf{Sidecar:} parallel-sidecar generated via
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (27 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-7.json}, 37KB).
No Greek phrases in this speech (consistent with the
Philippics generally). No date correction needed (Perseus
TEI numbering for Philippic 7 is regular, no anomaly; the
\texttt{date\_precision: month} in works.yaml correctly
reflects the manuscript-tradition month-precision dating,
since no sharper internal indication exists in the text).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 42.}
The meta entry was already correct, month-precision; Perseus
TEI numbering for Philippic 7 is regular (no anomaly
analogous to the Fam.\ 13.49 case).

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write
the shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write
contention; deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 1 \textit{parvis de rebus sed fortasse
necessariis consulimur}.} Rendered ``We are consulted on
small matters --- though perhaps necessary ones.''
\textit{Parvis $\ldots$ necessariis} is the deliberate
opening contrast: the day's business (Appian Way, Mint,
Lupercalia) is small, the larger crisis is not. The
\textit{sed fortasse} concedes the smallness without
dismissing it; the English needs to keep both poles
(``small $\ldots$ necessary'') in their balanced order.
\textit{Consulimur} as the technical Senate idiom ``we are
consulted'' (the presiding magistrate puts the question)
rather than the looser ``we are asked.''
\item \textbf{\S 1 \textit{animus aberrat a sententia
suspensus curis maioribus}.} Rendered ``my mind wanders
from the question, suspended on graver cares.'' \textit{Animus
aberrat} as ``my mind wanders'' (the standard Latin
metaphor, intelligible in English); \textit{a sententia} as
``from the question,'' the \textit{sententia} being the
matter on which one is to speak one's opinion (not the
opinion itself in this construction). \textit{Suspensus} as
``suspended,'' the participle of the mind hung between two
matters, the larger pulling on the smaller.
\item \textbf{\S 5 \textit{faveas tu hosti?} and
\textit{te consularem aut senatorem, denique civem
putes?}} The cascade of indignant rhetorical questions
about the man who would publish Antony's letters with
delight. Rendered \textit{would you favour an enemy?} and
\textit{after this think yourself a consular, or a senator,
or finally a citizen?} The subjunctive interrogatives kept
as ``would you,'' the climactic descent \textit{consularem
$\ldots$ senatorem $\ldots$ civem} kept in three steps,
the final \textit{civem} the lowest rung, since to be no
longer even a citizen is the full reproach.
\item \textbf{\S 8 \textit{ego ille $\ldots$ pacis semper
laudator, semper auctor, pacem cum M. Antonio esse nolo}.}
The \textit{propositio}, the architectural centre of the
speech. Rendered ``I, that man, I will say again and again,
always the praiser of peace, always its author, do not want
peace with Marcus Antonius.'' \textit{Ego ille} as ``I, that
man'' (the demonstrative is the rhetorical self-pointing, ``I,
that very same Cicero you all know to be the author of
peace''); \textit{laudator $\ldots$ auctor} kept as a
parallel pair (``praiser $\ldots$ author''); the closing
\textit{pacem $\ldots$ nolo} kept short and flat, as in the
Latin.
\item \textbf{\S 9 \textit{quia turpis est, quia periculosa,
quia esse non potest}.} The three reasons announced.
Rendered ``Because it is shameful, because it is dangerous,
because it cannot exist.'' \textit{Turpis} as ``shameful''
(the moral term, not ``ugly''); \textit{periculosa} as
``dangerous'' (plain); \textit{esse non potest} as ``it
cannot exist'' rather than ``it cannot be,'' since Cicero
is making the structural claim that no possible relation
between Antony and the Roman state can answer to the name
of peace.
\item \textbf{\S 10--13 \textit{non hostem iudicastis} ---
the five-fold anaphora}. Rendered with the repeating
formula ``did you not then judge Antony an enemy?'' / ``was
Antony not then judged an enemy?'' / etc. The English has to
keep the structural drumbeat, since the argument depends on
the listener feeling the iteration: each Senate decree was
an implicit declaration of enmity. \textit{Iudicare hostem}
as ``to judge an enemy'' (the technical formula by which the
Senate declared a citizen a public enemy), not ``to consider
him hostile.''
\item \textbf{\S 14 \textit{si non possumus facere $\ldots$
moriamur}.} The rhetorical apex. Rendered ``But if we cannot
do it --- I will say what is worthy of a senator and of a
Roman man --- let us die.'' \textit{Moriamur} as ``let us
die,'' the bare subjunctive, no padding (``let us perish''
would soften, ``let us die'' lands). The interjected
self-justification \textit{dicam quod dignum est et
senatore et Romano homine} kept exactly in position
between dashes, so the structure (the qualification, then
the verb) reads as in the Latin.
\item \textbf{\S 16 \textit{patronus quinque et triginta
tribuum $\ldots$ patronus centuriarum equitum Romanorum
$\ldots$ patronus eorum qui tribuni militares fuerunt
$\ldots$ patronus Iani medii}.} The four-fold
\textit{patronus} anaphora compressing Philippic 6's
statue-by-statue catalogue. Rendered ``patron of the
thirty-five tribes $\ldots$ patron of the centuries of
Roman equestrians $\ldots$ patron of those who were
military tribunes $\ldots$ patron of the Middle Janus.''
The English keeps \textit{patronus} $\to$ ``patron'' four
times, the structural drumbeat retained. The
intervening clauses (the \textit{suffragium sustulit}
about the equestrian voting reform) kept in their
subordinate position, so the four \textit{patronus}
heads remain audible.
\item \textbf{\S 17 \textit{Myrmillo in Asia depugnavit.
cum ornasset Thraecidicis comitem et familiarem suum,
illum miserum fugientem iugulavit, luculentam tamen ipse
plagam accepit, ut declarat cicatrix}.} The Mylasa arena
anecdote about Lucius Antonius. Rendered ``A Myrmillo, he
fought to the death in Asia. When he had armed his comrade
and intimate friend in the Thracian's gear, that wretch
fleeing he stabbed in the throat --- though he himself
took a luculent gash, as the scar declares.''
\textit{Myrmillo} kept as a transliterated proper noun (the
class of Roman gladiator, distinct from the
\textit{Thraex} class he was matched against);
\textit{depugnavit} as ``fought to the death'' (the
\textit{de-} prefix the technical term for a contest to
the finish, not a sparring bout); \textit{Thraecidicis}
as ``the Thracian's gear'' (the equipment of the
\textit{Thraex}); \textit{iugulavit} as ``stabbed in the
throat'' (the verb is anatomically specific);
\textit{luculenta plaga} kept as ``luculent gash,'' the
\textit{luculentus} adjective preserved as the Latinate
cognate the reader of expanded vocabulary will catch (a
``handsome'' wound, in the gallows-humour sense).
\item \textbf{\S 26 \textit{non illi senatus, sed ille
populo Romano bellum indixerit}.} The peroration's
brilliant reversal. Rendered ``then it is not the Senate
that has declared war on him, but he who has declared war
on the Roman people.'' \textit{Illi $\ldots$ ille} kept as
the contrast between the dative (war declared on him) and
the nominative (war declared by him); \textit{bellum
indicere} as the standard formal idiom ``to declare war,''
rendered as one phrase. The reversal is the architectural
hinge of the close: it is not we who are the warmakers but
the man we have already named an enemy in fact if not in
word.
\end{itemize}

\textit{Philippic 6} (4 January 43 BC, 19 sections,
$\sim$2{,}850 English words) end-to-end in one session: Latin
fetched (12{,}673 chars), English translated in
single-substantial-speech mode in four Edit chunks
(\textsection 1--5, 6--10, 11--15, 16--19), four-paragraph
headnote written (composition history --- the contio of
4 January 43 BC delivered to the people in the Forum three
days after \textit{Philippic 5}, after the Senate, against
Cicero's motion, had voted to send a legation of Sulpicius
Rufus, Piso, and Philippus to Antony at Mutina rather than
declare \textit{tumultus} outright --- and the speech's three-
part shape: \S 1--9 the report and case against the legation,
\S 10--15 the catalogue of Lucius Antonius's statues set up
by the thirty-five tribes, the equestrians, the military
tribunes, and the moneylenders of the Middle Janus, \S 16--19
the close on \textit{libertas}). Status flipped
\texttt{pending}~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted};

sidecar (19 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-6.json}, 30KB);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} and \texttt{validate.py} both run
clean (8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at 41 pending earlier-dated works, down from 42
