drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares}
10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains the marker, since
\textit{Philippic 6} is dated 4 January 43 BC and so falls
inside the chronological-gap zone.

\textbf{Cowork session 41 --- 1 substantial speech completed
(PM-direct, 19 sections):}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{PM-direct (\textit{Philippic 6}, 4 January 43 BC,
the popular damage-control speech delivered to the people in
the Forum three days after the Kalends debate, reporting on
the compromise legation):} 19 sections, $\sim$2{,}850 English
words. A short \textit{contio} in three unequal parts. The
first half (\S 1--9) is Cicero making the best of a Senate
vote he opposed: the legation is at bottom a denunciation of
war, like the one once sent to Hannibal not to attack
Saguntum (\S 4, 6); Antony will not obey, because he has
never obeyed his own judgment let alone the Senate's
(\S 4--5); Decimus Brutus is the test, a Brutus born for
liberty (\S 8--9). The middle (\S 10--15) is the famous
set-piece on Lucius Antonius the murmillo brother, the
\textit{Africanus} of the Antonine inner circle, ridiculed
through his four (or five) public statues: the gilded
equestrian \textit{Of the thirty-five tribes to their patron}
(\S 12), the Forum statue beside that of Quintus Tremulus who
conquered the Hernici (\S 13), the equestrian-order statue
(\S 13), the military-tribunes' statue erected by men twice in
Caesar's army (\S 14), and the \textit{palmaris}
moneylenders' statue from the Middle Janus inscribed \textit{To
Lucius Antonius, from the Middle Janus, their patron} (\S 15)
--- with the Trebellius \textit{O fides!} digression
(\S 11) as bridge. The close (\S 16--19) is the great
peroration on liberty: \textit{populum Romanum servire fas non
est, quem di immortales omnibus gentibus imperare voluerunt
$\ldots$ aliae nationes servitutem pati possunt, populi
Romani est propria libertas} (\S 19). \textbf{Sidecar:}
parallel-sidecar generated via
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (19 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-6.json}, 30KB).
No Greek phrases in this speech (consistent with the
Philippics generally; the closing \textit{libertas} sentence
is purely Latin). No date correction needed
(\texttt{-0043-01-04} day-precision was already correct).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 41.}
The meta entry was already correct, day-precision; Perseus
TEI numbering for Philippic 6 is regular (no anomaly
analogous to the Fam.\ 13.49 case).

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write
the shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write
contention; deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 1 \textit{minus quidem illa severe quam
decuit, non tamen omnino dissolute}.} Rendered ``less
severely, indeed, than was fitting, but still not altogether
weakly.'' \textit{Dissolute} is the contrast term to
\textit{severe} and means here ``slackly, weakly'' (the
\textit{dissolutus} as the man without resolve, not the
\textit{dissolutus} as the wastrel). The judgment is the
controlled, slightly bitter one Cicero uses repeatedly through
the speech --- restated at \S 7 with \textit{non omnino
dissolutum est quod decrevit senatus: habet atrocitatis
aliquid legatio} --- the bicolon of \textit{severe} versus
\textit{dissolute} the architecture of the whole speech. The
English needs to keep both poles intact.
\item \textbf{\S 2 \textit{ut meminissem quam personam
impositam a vobis sustinerem}.} The theatrical metaphor of
\textit{persona} (the actor's mask). Rendered ``so that I
might remember what part you had imposed upon me to
sustain.'' \textit{Persona} as ``part'' (the actor's
function, not the modern ``person''); \textit{impositam $\ldots$
sustinerem} kept as a unit, the \textit{sustinere personam}
being the standard idiom for ``to sustain a role.''
\item \textbf{\S 3 \textit{quam ob rem $\ldots$ quae vobis
potest cum hoc gladiatore condicionis, aequitatis, legationis
esse communitas?}} The closing tricolon on the negotiation
with Antony. Rendered ``What common ground of terms, of fair
dealing, of negotiation can you have with this gladiator?''
\textit{Condicio} as ``terms'' (the technical term for terms
of agreement, not ``condition''); \textit{aequitas} as ``fair
dealing'' (the procedural-fairness sense, not modern
``equality''); \textit{legatio} as ``negotiation'' (the
function of an embassy, not its bare name, since the legation
\textit{is} what's at issue). \textit{Gladiator} kept literal
--- the abuse-word recurring through Philippics 2 and 3.
\item \textbf{\S 4 \textit{semper eo tractus est quo libido
rapuit, quo levitas, quo furor, quo vinolentia}.} The four-
part anaphora of \textit{quo}. Rendered ``He has always been
dragged where lust dragged him, where fickleness, where
frenzy, where drunkenness.'' The English keeps \textit{quo}
$\to$ ``where'' four times; \textit{libido / levitas / furor /
vinolentia} as ``lust / fickleness / frenzy / drunkenness'' ---
each a single English noun, no doubling, since the rhythm of
the Latin is the architecture and the English nouns need to
land singly to preserve it. \textit{Vinolentia} specifically
as ``drunkenness'' (not ``love of wine'' or ``intoxication'')
to give it the same monosyllabic weight as \textit{furor}.
\item \textbf{\S 7 \textit{nec vero de illo sicut de homine
aliquo debemus, sed ut de importunissima belua cogitare}.}
Rendered ``Nor indeed ought we to think of him as we do of
any human being, but as of some most savage beast.''
\textit{Importuna belua} is the bestiality-of-Antony figure
that runs through the speech and culminates in the Hannibal-
beast comparison of Philippic 4. \textit{Importunissimus} as
``most savage'' (rather than ``inopportune'' or
``troublesome,'' which miss the predatory force); \textit{belua}
as ``beast'' kept simple. The whole structure --- ``not as of
a man, but as of a beast'' --- preserved as a contrast pair.
\item \textbf{\S 10 \textit{nam hic inter illos Africanus
est}.} Of Lucius Antonius among Antony's lieutenants.
Rendered ``For this man, among those of Antony's circle, is
an Africanus.'' \textit{Africanus} kept as the bare cognomen
--- the sarcasm depends on the reader hearing the name of
the greatest Roman general and seeing it applied to a
gladiator who killed his friend at Mylasa. To translate
\textit{Africanus} as ``a chief'' or ``a paragon'' would
flatten the irony; the Latin name carries it in English too.
\item \textbf{\S 11 \textit{O fide! --- hoc enim opinor
Trebellium sumpsisse cognomen}.} The Trebellius pun.
Rendered ``O Faith! --- for this, I think, is the cognomen
Trebellius assumed.'' The joke is that Trebellius's cognomen
sounds nothing like \textit{fides}; Cicero pretends it does,
sarcastically, since Trebellius has acted in every way
opposite to faith. The English needs to preserve the joke's
shape (\textit{O fides!} as exclamation, the supposed
etymology, the punchline of the defrauded creditors), which
it does if \textit{fides} is translated as ``faith'' rather
than ``credit'' or ``loyalty'' --- ``faith'' lets the
cognomen-pun land, ``credit'' wouldn't.
\item \textbf{\S 12 inscription \textit{Qvinqve et triginta
tribvs patrono} and \S 15 inscription \textit{L.\ Antonio a
Iano medio patrono}.} The two statue inscriptions. Rendered
in \textit{italic} (``To their patron from the thirty-five
tribes,'' ``To Lucius Antonius, from the Middle Janus, their
patron'') to mark them as quoted inscriptions rather than
Cicero's own voice. The \textit{equo publico} equestrian
inscription at \S 13 (\textit{patrono}) likewise marked
\textit{italic}.
\item \textbf{\S 15 \textit{quis umquam in illo Iano inventus
est qui L.\ Antonio mille nummum ferret expensum?}} The
moneylender joke. Rendered ``Who was ever found in that
Janus to enter on his books a thousand sesterces lent to
Lucius Antonius?'' \textit{Expensum ferre} is the technical
accounting idiom of the Roman moneylender --- to record on
the credit side of one's ledger an amount paid out as a loan
--- so the English needs to land in the bookkeeping register
(``enter on his books $\ldots$ lent'') rather than collapse to
``lend.'' The point is that no one in the Middle Janus would
have lent Lucius Antonius a sesterce; the statue inscribing
him as patron of the place is therefore self-evidently absurd.
\item \textbf{\S 17 \textit{si pacem adferent, cupidum me; si
bellum, providum iudicatote}.} The pivot couplet. Rendered
``if they bring peace, judge me eager for it; if war, judge
me foreseeing.'' \textit{Cupidum / providum} as ``eager /
foreseeing'' --- the contrast is between desire and
foreknowledge, both Latin words ending in \textit{-um}; the
English ``eager / foreseeing'' is the closest balance
available, the parallel weakened only slightly by the change
of suffix. \textit{Iudicatote} (future imperative) preserved
as ``judge'' rather than collapsed to a flat indicative.
\item \textbf{\S 19 \textit{populum Romanum servire fas non
est, quem di immortales omnibus gentibus imperare voluerunt}.}
The peroration. Rendered ``It is not divinely lawful for the
Roman people to be slaves --- whom the immortal gods willed
to rule over all nations.'' \textit{Fas non est} as ``it is
not divinely lawful'' (rather than ``it is not right'' or
``it is not lawful'') to preserve the religious-civic charge:
\textit{fas} is the divine-law term, and Cicero is invoking
the gods explicitly in the next clause. The closing
\textit{aliae nationes servitutem pati possunt, populi Romani
est propria libertas} kept as a balanced bicolon (``Other
nations can bear slavery; liberty belongs to the Roman people
by right''), with \textit{propria} as ``by right'' rather than
``their own'' --- the property-language sense of
\textit{proprius}, what belongs to one's nature and not to
another.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-40):} \textbf{910 /
958 works drafted (\~95.0\%)} --- session 40 completed
\textit{Philippic 5} (1 January 43 BC, 53 sections,
$\sim$5{,}900 English words) end-to-end in one session: Latin
fetched (37{,}465 chars), English translated in
single-substantial-speech mode in eleven Edit chunks of five
sections each (with a final three-section closing chunk
\textsection 51--53), four-paragraph headnote written
(composition history --- the inaugural Senate meeting of Pansa
and Hirtius in the temple of Concord on the Kalends, the
Calenus proposal to send a legation to Antony rather than
proceed to war, Cicero's rebuttal --- and the speech's
two-half structure: \S 1--34 the case against the embassy and
for war, with \S 7--16 the long catalogue of Antony's
consular crimes culminating in the third-panel-of-judges
\textit{set piece}, \S 17--22 the armed gangs and the
Brundisium centurion-killings, \S 23--25 the Hannibal
comparison, \S 26--34 the practical case and Cicero's motion
of \textit{tumultus}/\textit{iustitium}/\textit{saga}/\textit{dilectus};
then \S 35--53 the second half, the drafted senatus consulta
honouring Decimus Brutus, Lepidus, Octavian (\S 42--51, the
longest single block of the speech, including \textit{
promitto, recipio, spondeo} as Cicero's personal guarantee of
Caesar's future conduct), Egnatuleius, and the veterans of
the Martian and Fourth Legions). Status flipped \texttt{pending}
~$\rightarrow$~\texttt{drafted}; \texttt{backfill\_structural.py}
regenerated the parallel sidecar (53 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-5.json}, 87KB);
\texttt{build\_manifest.py} and \texttt{validate.py} both run
clean (8 warnings: pre-existing orphan-Latin for
\texttt{054bc-ad-familiares-13-49} plus the chronological-gap
warning, now at 42 pending earlier-dated works, down from 43
post-session-39, the expected $-1$ decrement). \textbf{Latest
drafted by deep chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares}

\textit{Philippic 5} is dated 1 January 43 BC and so falls
inside the chronological-gap zone.

\textbf{Cowork session 40 --- 1 substantial speech completed
(PM-direct, 53 sections):}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{PM-direct (\textit{Philippic 5}, 1 January 43
BC, the inaugural-Senate-meeting speech of the consular year
of Pansa and Hirtius, the great rebuttal of the Calenus
embassy proposal):} 53 sections, $\sim$5{,}900 English words.
The longest Philippic so far in the cycle, and the most
legislative in shape: not the indignation of Philippic 4 nor
the formal motion of Philippic 3, but a sustained debate-
speech that begins with the case against sending a legation
to Antony, runs through Antony's whole record of public
violence as consul, and closes with a series of drafted
senatus consulta honouring those already in arms. The two
halves are unequal --- \textsection 1--34 against the
embassy and for war (with the great extended caricature of
Antony's third panel of judges at \S 12--16, Cydas of Crete
and Lysiades the Athenian named, and the cascading practical
argument at \S 26--34 that a legation will quench the fire of
the Roman people and break the spirit of the municipalities);
then \S 35--53 the legislative honours --- Decimus Brutus
(praise for holding Gaul), Lepidus (a gilded equestrian
statue for restoring Sextus Pompey), Gaius Caesar at length
(\S 42--51, propraetorian imperium and a seat in the Senate
with consular rank, with Alexander and Africanus and
Flamininus brought in to defend the unprecedented honours),
Egnatuleius (three years before the legal age), and the
veterans of the Martian and Fourth Legions (\S 53, the long
final s.c.). \textbf{Sidecar:} parallel-sidecar generated via
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (53 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/043bc-philippic-5.json}, 87KB).
No Greek phrases in this speech (consistent with the
Philippics generally). No date correction needed
(\texttt{-0043-01-01} day-precision was already correct).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 40.}
The meta entry was already correct, day-precision.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 3 \textit{non iam levitatis est, sed, ut quod
sentio dicam, dementiae}.} Rendered ``is no longer mere
fickleness, but, to speak my mind, derangement.''
\textit{Levitas} is the lighter charge (mere inconsistency
between the s.c.s of 20 December and a legation now);
\textit{dementia} is the heavier --- not ``madness'' (which
in English drains the legal-political charge) but the
faculty-of-mind term ``derangement.'' Cicero is gradating
the indictment, and the English nouns need to gradate too.
\item \textbf{\S 5 \textit{hoc qui non videt, excors, qui cum
videt decernit, impius est}.} The closing dichotomy on giving
Antony Plancus's Gaul. Rendered ``He who does not see this is
brainless; he who, seeing, decrees it, is impious.'' \textit{
Excors} as ``brainless'' (rather than ``heartless,'' which
mistakes the etymology --- \textit{cor} here as the seat of
intelligence, not affection); \textit{impius} kept as
``impious,'' which preserves the religious-civic weight that
the simple ``wicked'' or ``unprincipled'' would lose.
\item \textbf{\S 12--16 the third panel of judges (\textit{
tertia decuria}).} The most savagely funny set-piece in the
speech, naming Cydas of Crete and Lysiades the Athenian (the
son of Phaedrus the Epicurean), and ridiculing the Greeks,
gamblers, exiles, dancers, and lyre-players whom Antony has
filed with the treasury as iudices. Rendered with the cast of
characters preserved by name (\textit{Cydam amo Cretensem,
portentum insulae} as ``I am fond of Cydas of Crete, the
prodigy of his island''; \textit{ne Curium quidem esse
crudelem qui periculum fortunae cotidie facit} as ``Curius is
not cruel either, who takes his chance with Fortune every
day,'' preserving the gambling joke). \textit{Pallium /
toga} kept as ``pallium and toga'' (the Greek cloak and the
Roman, with no English equivalent that lands the cultural
contrast).
\item \textbf{\S 17 \textit{Cinnam memini; vidi Sullam; modo
Caesarem}.} The tricolon of past strongmen. Rendered ``I
remember Cinna; I saw Sulla; only the other day, Caesar.''
The three verbs gradate from \textit{memini} (childhood
memory --- Cicero was eight when Cinna was killed in 84) to
\textit{vidi} (his late teens and twenties, when Sulla was
dictator) to the bare \textit{modo} for the recent Caesar:
preserved as ``remember,'' ``saw,'' and an unmediated bare
noun, which lands the increasing immediacy without forcing
the verb sequence into English.
\item \textbf{\S 25 \textit{ergo Hannibal hostis, civis
Antonius?}} The pivot of the first half. Rendered ``Is
Hannibal then an enemy, and Antony a citizen?'' The
asyndetic Latin (no copula, no ``then'' in the question)
opened up with ``Is~$\ldots$~then~$\ldots$~and~$\ldots$'' for
English clarity, but the rhetorical pair \textit{hostis /
civis} kept verbatim and the question form preserved. The
whole inferential structure of the section --- Hannibal was
restrained by self-interest, Antony's faction is not ---
preserved without softening.
\item \textbf{\S 31 \textit{tumultum decerni, iustitium
edici, saga sumi dico oportere, dilectum haberi sublatis
vacationibus}.} The four-part motion. Rendered ``I say that
a state of tumult must be decreed, the courts suspended,
the military cloaks taken up, a levy held, exemptions
revoked.'' \textit{Tumultus} kept literal (``state of
tumult''), since it is the technical term for the formal
declaration that an emergency in Italy or Gaul exists;
\textit{iustitium} as ``the courts suspended'' (rather than
``a iustitium declared,'' which would over-Latinise);
\textit{saga sumere} as ``take up the military cloaks''
(the standard idiom for the change from the civilian toga
to the military sagum); \textit{dilectus} kept as ``levy.''
\item \textbf{\S 38 \textit{cum Antonio diadema Caesari
imponente se avertit gemituque et maestitia declaravit}.}
Lepidus's reaction at the Lupercalia. Rendered ``when, while
Antony was placing the diadem on Caesar, he turned aside and
by his groans and his sadness made clear $\ldots$'' --- the
present participle \textit{imponente} kept as a temporal
``while~$\ldots$ was placing'' (rather than collapsing to
``Antony's offering of the diadem,'' which would lose the
moment of action). The whole passage is a careful
exoneration of Lepidus, set up to license the gilded statue
in \S 41.
\item \textbf{\S 51 \textit{promitto, recipio, spondeo,
patres conscripti}.} Cicero's personal guarantee of the
young Caesar's future conduct. Rendered ``I promise, I
undertake, I pledge, senators.'' Three Latin verbs $\to$
three English verbs, each weightier than the last --- the
\textit{tricolon crescendo} from a private undertaking
(\textit{promitto}) through assuming-the-responsibility
(\textit{recipio}) to the formal solemn pledge with
religious weight (\textit{spondeo}, the verb of sponsion).
The reader who knows what the next eighteen months will
do to this guarantee should be allowed to feel its
solemnity intact, not have it softened.
\item \textbf{\S 53 the closing senatus consultum on the
Martian and Fourth Legions.} Reproduced as a single
unbroken legislative period in English (no break-out of
sub-clauses) so that the cumulative weight of the package
of exemptions, lands, and bonuses --- and the procedural
device that the consuls Pansa and Hirtius are to draw up
the details \textit{alter ambove, si eis videretur} ---
lands as a single drafted s.c. The closing \textit{dixi ad
ea omnia, consules, de quibus rettulistis}: rendered ``I
have spoken, consuls, on all those matters concerning
which you have referred to us,'' the senatorial-protocol
formula that closes the contribution.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Suggested next translation batch} (when session 40 is
landed via \texttt{bash scripts/cowork\_handoff.sh "session 40:
\ldots"} and session 41 opens):

\textbf{Session 41 opens \textit{Philippic 6} (4 January 43 BC,
$\sim$19 sections).} A short \textit{contio} speech delivered
to the people in the Forum on 4 January 43, three days after
Philippic 5, after the Senate had ended by sending the
legation (Sulpicius, Piso, Philippus) to Antony despite
Cicero's opposition --- the compromise outcome that Philippic
5 had argued against. The speech is Cicero's report to the
people of what was decided, of what was decided differently
from what he had urged, and of why the people should
nevertheless not be discouraged: war is still coming, the
legation is only a brief delay, the Senate's resolve is firm.
Short --- $\sim$19 sections fits comfortably in a single
session in single-substantial-speech mode, three to four
5-section Edit chunks.

\textbf{After \textit{Philippic 6}} (sessions 42--49 or so):
\textit{7} (Jan 43, $\sim$27 sections), \textit{8} (3 Feb 43,
$\sim$33 sections), \textit{9} (4 Feb 43, $\sim$17 sections),
\textit{10} (Feb 43, $\sim$26 sections), \textit{11} (Mar 43,
$\sim$40 sections), \textit{12} (Mar 43, $\sim$30 sections),
\textit{13} (20 Mar 43, $\sim$50 sections), \textit{14} (21 Apr
43, $\sim$38 sections). Each fits a single session (with the
longer two --- 11 and 13 --- nearer the upper bound). Total
Philippic phase is likely 9 sessions left (Phil 3, 4, and 5
now completed, Phil 6--14 to go).

\textbf{After the Philippics} (sessions $\sim$50--60 or so):
sweep the major philosophical treatises (\textit{Cato Maior
De Senectute}, \textit{Paradoxa Stoicorum}, \textit{Academica},
\textit{De Finibus}, \textit{Timaeus}, \textit{De Natura
Deorum}, \textit{Tusculanae Disputationes}, \textit{De
Divinatione}, \textit{De Fato}, \textit{Laelius De Amicitia},
\textit{De Officiis}). The remaining minor speeches and the
rhetorica (\textit{partitiones-oratoriae}, \textit{brutus},
\textit{de-optimo-genere-oratorum}, \textit{orator},
\textit{topica}) are slotted alongside as one-session pickups.

sequence with Cicero descending from the Curia to the Rostra ---

\textbf{Cowork session 39 --- 1 substantial speech completed
(1 worker / PM direct, 16 sections):}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{PM-direct (\textit{Philippic 4}, 20 December 44
BC, the popular counterpart to the Third Philippic delivered
to the people in the Forum on the very same day, only hours
after the senatorial address):} 16 sections, $\sim$2{,}180
English words. The Senate meeting in the morning carried
Cicero's motion that Octavian, the Martian and Fourth Legions,
Lucius Egnatuleius, Decimus Brutus, and the Gallic towns be
commended for their actions against Antony. Cicero then went
down into the Forum and announced the result to the crowd that
had gathered there. The whole speech is built around a single
proposition stated in \S 1: \textit{est hostis a senatu nondum
verbo appellatus, sed re iam iudicatus Antonius} (``Antony has
not yet been called an enemy by the Senate in word, but in
fact he has already been so judged''). \S 1--6 the inferential
chain: armies have rightly been raised against Antony; the
Senate has just praised those who raised them; therefore by
inescapable inference the Senate has judged him an enemy ---
and the Martian and Fourth Legions, in deserting him for
Octavian and taking their stand at Alba, judged him an enemy
before the Senate did. \S 7--9 the converging judgments of
Decimus Brutus, the province of Gaul, all Italy, and the
people themselves. \S 10 the gods --- prodigies and a universal
consent which cannot have been without divine prompting. \S 11
--16 the hortatory close: Antony is no longer a wicked man but
a beast in a pit (\textit{immani taetraque belua quae, quoniam
in foveam incidit, obruatur}, \S 12); the contest is with an
assassin, a brigand, a Spartacus; the comparison with Catiline
replaces the Tarquin comparison of the Third Philippic and is
sharpened into a boast (Catiline at least raised an army from
nothing, whereas Antony has lost the army he was given);
\textit{me auctore et principe} closes on Cicero's own role
after the long withdrawal. \textbf{Sidecar:} parallel-sidecar
generated via \texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (16 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/044bc-philippic-4.json}, 23KB).
No Greek phrases in this speech (consistent with the Philippics
generally). No date correction needed (\texttt{-0044-12-20}
day-precision was already correct).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 39.}
The meta entry was already correct, day-precision.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\textit{Quirites}, address throughout.} Rendered
``citizens'' consistently, parallel to the rendering of
\textit{patres conscripti} as ``senators.'' \textit{Quirites}
is the formal civic address to the people in their civilian
capacity, and the modern English ``citizens'' lands the
register without the antiquarian flavour of ``Romans'' or
``men of Rome.'' The choice carries through every speech to
the people; flagged here as a recurring decision rather than
a per-passage crux.
\item \textbf{\S 1 \textit{est hostis a senatu nondum verbo
appellatus, sed re iam iudicatus Antonius}.} The thesis
sentence of the whole speech. Rendered ``Antony has not yet
been called an enemy by the Senate in word, but in fact he has
already been so judged.'' The \textit{verbo / re} antithesis
(in word vs.\ in fact) preserved as ``in word / in fact''; the
\textit{nondum~$\ldots$~iam} pair (not yet~$\ldots$~already)
preserved. \textit{Hostis} rendered as the technical ``enemy''
(public enemy) rather than the bare ``foe'' --- though here,
unlike in Philippic 3 \S 14, ``public enemy'' would over-
specify and pre-empt the inferential argument; the simple
``enemy'' lets the inference build to its conclusion.
\item \textbf{\S 3 \textit{nomen clarissimi adulescentis vel
pueri potius; sunt enim facta eius immortalitatis, nomen
aetatis}.} Rendered ``the name of that most illustrious young
man --- or boy, rather; for his deeds are those of
immortality, his name those of his age.'' The
\textit{adulescentis / pueri potius} self-correction echoes
the famous \textit{adulescens, paene potius puer} of
Philippic 3 \S 3 and is preserved; the \textit{facta
immortalitatis / nomen aetatis} chiasmus (deeds belong to
immortality, name to youth) kept by parallel English nouns.
\item \textbf{\S 5 \textit{legio Martia, quae mihi videtur
divinitus ab eo deo traxisse nomen a quo populum Romanum
generatum accepimus}.} The etymological play on the Martian
Legion's name (Mars, ancestor of Romulus and so of the Roman
people). Rendered ``the Martian Legion --- which seems to me
to have drawn its name, by divine appointment, from that god
from whom we are taught the Roman people was begotten.''
\textit{Divinitus} as ``by divine appointment'' (rather than
``divinely'' or ``by the gods,'' which would either weaken or
over-specify); the legion's name is not glossed as ``from
Mars'' because the rhetorical figure is the periphrastic
identification, and to gloss it is to deflate it.
\item \textbf{\S 9 \textit{negat hoc D.~Brutus imperator,
consul designatus, natus rei publicae civis; negat Gallia,
negat cuncta Italia, negat senatus, negatis vos}.} The
anaphoric tricolon-plus-two on \textit{negat}. Rendered
``Decimus Brutus, commander, consul-elect, a citizen born for
the commonwealth, denies this; Gaul denies it; all Italy
denies it; the Senate denies it; you deny it.'' Five
\textit{negat/negatis} → five English ``denies'' (one ``deny''
on the second-person plural to match the form-shift). The
cascade of subjects (from Decimus Brutus through Gaul and
Italy and the Senate to the addressed crowd) is the load-
bearing rhetorical figure and is preserved verbatim.
\item \textbf{\S 12 \textit{immani taetraque belua quae,
quoniam in foveam incidit, obruatur}.} The beast-in-a-pit
image. Rendered ``a vast and foul beast which, since it has
fallen into the pit, must be buried under it.'' The
\textit{fovea} (the pit dug by hunters to trap large game)
kept as ``the pit'' (definite article: the trap that the
hunting metaphor implies); \textit{obruatur} as ``must be
buried under it'' rather than ``crushed'' or ``overwhelmed,''
to keep the physical specificity of the hunters' fill-in-
the-pit-with-stones gesture, which is the load-bearing image
of the second clause (the next sentence \textit{si enim illim
emerserit} --- ``if he climbs out of there'' --- depends on
the literal pit).
\item \textbf{\S 13 \textit{Alia omnia falsa, incerta sunt,
caduca, mobilia: virtus est una altissimis defixa radicibus,
quae numquam vi ulla labefactari potest, numquam demoveri
loco}.} The contrast of false-perishable things with valour-
rooted-deep. Rendered ``All other things are false, uncertain,
perishable, shifting: valour alone is set with the deepest
roots; she can never by any force be shaken, never moved from
her place.'' The four-adjective catalogue (\textit{falsa,
incerta, caduca, mobilia}) preserved as four adjectives; the
\textit{numquam~$\ldots$~numquam} anaphora preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 14 \textit{senatum, id est orbis terrae
consilium, delere gestit, ipse consilium publicum nullum
habet}.} The Senate-as-council-of-the-world image. Rendered
``he longs to destroy the Senate (that is, the council of the
whole world), while himself having no public council.''
\textit{Orbis terrae consilium} kept literally; the parenthesis
``(that is, $\ldots$)'' for the \textit{id est} apposition
makes the figure self-glossing for the reader. The
\textit{consilium}-pun (the Senate as \textit{consilium}, vs.\
Antony's lack of any \textit{consilium publicum}) preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 15 \textit{omne certamen cum percussore, cum
latrone, cum Spartaco}.} The triple of insulting names.
Rendered ``with an assassin, with a brigand, with a Spartacus.''
\textit{Percussor} kept as ``assassin'' (not ``murderer,''
which loses the political-killing flavour); \textit{latro} as
``brigand'' (a recurring Ciceronian term for Antony and his
followers, distinct from the more general ``robber'');
\textit{Spartacus} kept as a proper name with the indefinite
article (``a Spartacus''), which renders the noun-of-type idiom
better than the bare proper name and was a Ciceronian
commonplace.
\item \textbf{\S 16 \textit{longo intervallo me auctore et
principe ad spem libertatis exarsimus}.} The closing cadence,
echoing the Third Philippic \S 28 \textit{hodierno die primum,
patres conscripti, longo intervallo in possessionem libertatis
pedem ponimus}. Rendered ``for the first time after a long
interval, with me as proposer and leader, we have caught fire
with the hope of liberty.'' \textit{Exarsimus} as ``caught
fire'' (rather than ``blazed up,'' which over-translates the
inceptive); \textit{me auctore et principe} kept literal,
since both \textit{auctor} (the proposer of the motion) and
\textit{princeps} (the leading-from-the-front leader) are
load-bearing technical terms that the speech has earned.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Session 40 opens \textit{Philippic 5} (1 January 43 BC,
$\sim$53 sections).} The first speech of the consular year of
Pansa and Hirtius, delivered at the inaugural Senate meeting.
This is the long debate-speech opposing the Calenus proposal
of an embassy to Antony; the centrepiece is the case for war
on Antony rather than negotiation, and the speech also moves
the formal extraordinary honours for Octavian (the imperium
and the legions' confirmation) and for Decimus Brutus.
Substantial --- $\sim$53 sections is towards the upper bound
for one session; the single-substantial-speech pattern
continues, with the speech translated in $\sim$5-section
chunks (so $\sim$11 chunks across the session), WIP commits at
chunk boundaries, headnote at close. If the session budget
runs short, a clean stop at a chunk boundary (e.g.\ \S 25 or
\S 30) and a second session to finish is the documented
escape hatch.

\textbf{Cowork session 38 --- 1 substantial speech completed
(1 worker, 39 sections):}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Worker A (\textit{Philippic 3}, 20 December 44 BC,
the first Philippic delivered after Octavian's break with Antony
and the speech that turned a private quarrel into a Senate-led
war):} 39 sections, $\sim$5{,}900 English words. The Senate was
called by the tribunes of the plebs ostensibly to provide a guard
for the consuls-elect (Pansa and Hirtius, due to enter office on
the Kalends of January). Cicero, who since the Second Philippic
had withdrawn from public business, came in person and turned
the agenda. \S 1--7 the praise of Octavian (\textit{C. Caesar
adulescens, paene potius puer, incredibili ac divina quadam
mente atque virtute}, \S 3) and of the Martian Legion at Alba
under Lucius Egnatuleius and the Fourth Legion's defection.
\S 8--12 the Decimus Brutus / Tarquin comparison: the elder
Brutus expelled a proud king, the younger Brutus shuts out a
king who is cruel, impious, a seller of immunities and
citizenships and provinces. \S 13--14 the consensus of the towns
of Gaul, with the climactic resolution that Antony be judged
``not by word but in fact, not only no consul but a public
enemy.'' \S 15--18 the savage cross-comparison of Antony's
attack on Octavian's mother ``from Aricia'' against Antony's own
wife Fulvia, whose father Bambalio got his cognomen from his
stammer (\textit{cognomen ex contumelia traxerit}). \S 19--24
the collapse of the Senate meeting of 28 November --- the edict
threatening absentees, the \textit{ipse non adfuit} retort, the
mockery of Antony's broken syntax (\textit{en cur magister eius
ex oratore arator factus sit}, \S 22), the news of the Fourth
Legion's defection and the war-cloak flight (\textit{quam misera
fuga, quam foeda, quam turpis}, \S 24). \S 25--27 the catalogue
of consulars who refused the provinces Antony had allotted.
\S 28--36 the hortatory close --- \textit{hodierno die primum,
patres conscripti, longo intervallo in possessionem libertatis
pedem ponimus} (\S 28) and the choice (\S 29) \textit{aut
libertatem propriam Romani et generis et nominis recuperemus
aut mortem servituti anteponamus}. \S 37--39 the motion proper:
guards for the Kalends of January, formal commendation of
Decimus Brutus, the provinces held under the Julian Law, honours
for Octavian and the legions to be referred by the incoming
consuls. \textbf{Sidecar:} parallel-sidecar generated via
\texttt{backfill\_structural.py} (39 segments,
\texttt{data/parallel/speeches/044bc-philippic-3.json}, 63KB).
No Greek phrases in this speech (consistent with the Philippics
generally, which are senatorial in register and bare of the
Greek-tagged informalities Cicero uses with Atticus). No date
correction needed (\texttt{-0044-12-20} day-precision was
already correct).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 38.}
The meta entry was already correct, day-precision.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 3 \textit{C. Caesar adulescens, paene potius
puer, incredibili ac divina quadam mente atque virtute}.}
Rendered ``Gaius Caesar, a young man --- almost still a boy ---
with an incredible and almost divine mind and courage.''
Preserved the \textit{paene potius puer} self-correction
(Cicero shifting from \textit{adulescens} to the stronger
\textit{puer}); the \textit{divina quadam} kept as ``almost
divine,'' with \textit{quadam} as ``almost'' rather than the
weaker ``some sort of.''
\item \textbf{\S 3 \textit{non enim effudit: in salute rei
publicae conlocavit}.} The famous self-correction (Octavian did
not ``pour out'' his patrimony --- he ``invested'' it). Rendered
``he did not pour it out: he invested it in the safety of the
commonwealth.'' The \textit{effudit / conlocavit} antithesis
(squandering vs. investment) preserved as ``poured out /
invested.''
\item \textbf{\S 8 \textit{O civem natum rei publicae, memorem
sui nominis imitatoremque maiorum!}} Apostrophe to Decimus
Brutus. Rendered ``O citizen born for the commonwealth, mindful
of his name, an imitator of his ancestors!'' The
\textit{memorem / imitatorem} doublet preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 9 \textit{L. Brutus regem superbum non tulit:
D. Brutus sceleratum atque impium regnare patietur Antonium?}}
The two Bruti antithesis. Rendered ``Lucius Brutus would not
endure a proud king: shall Decimus Brutus suffer the criminal
and impious Antony to be king?'' The proper-name parallelism
and the \textit{tulit / patietur} verb-pair preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 10 \textit{huius domi inter quasilla
pendebatur aurum, numerabatur pecunia}.} The wool-basket
image. Rendered ``in this man's house gold was weighed out
among the wool-baskets, money was counted out.'' Kept the
literal \textit{quasilla} (wool-baskets), which is the load-
bearing detail --- Antony's house is run like a women's
work-room, with gold weighed beside the spinning baskets ---
not generalised to ``in his household.''
\item \textbf{\S 14 \textit{non verbo, sed re, non modo non
consul sed etiam hostis Antonius}.} The climactic resolution.
Rendered ``not by word, but in fact, not only no consul but
also a public enemy.'' The double \textit{non $\ldots$ sed}
construction preserved in both members, and \textit{hostis}
rendered as the technical ``public enemy'' rather than the bare
``enemy'' (since the speech's whole point is to push the Senate
toward, but stop just short of, the formal \textit{hostis}
declaration).
\item \textbf{\S 16 \textit{Bambalio quidam pater, homo nullo
numero $\ldots$ cognomen ex contumelia traxerit}.} The savage
attack on Fulvia's father. Rendered ``the father was a certain
Bambalio, a man of no account whatever $\ldots$ got his
cognomen out of insult, on account of the hesitancy of his
tongue and the stupor of his heart.'' \textit{Bambalio} is
itself an onomatopoeic stammer-word (cf.\ Greek
\textit{bambalein}, to chatter the teeth); the
\textit{haesitantia linguae / stupor cordis} doublet preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 16 \textit{vellem hanc contemptionem
pecuniae suis reliquisset!}} The dry aside about Tuditanus.
Rendered ``I could wish he had left his descendants this
contempt for money!'' The understatement (Antony and his
relatives have plainly not inherited the trait) kept implicit,
not glossed.
\item \textbf{\S 21 \textit{quem in edictis Spartacum
appellat, hunc in senatu ne improbum quidem dicere audet}.}
Rendered ``Whom in his edicts he calls a Spartacus, in the
Senate he does not dare even call dishonest.'' The
\textit{Spartacum / improbum} antithesis (extreme insult in
print, no insult at all in person) preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 22 \textit{en cur magister eius ex oratore
arator factus sit}.} The pun on Antony's teacher
(Sextus Clodius?) being given two thousand acres of public
land in the Leontine plain. \textit{Orator / arator} (one
letter's difference, but the whole shift from rhetoric to
ploughing). Rendered ``see why his teacher has been turned from
an orator into a ploughman.'' Considered ``orator into a
tiller,'' but \textit{arator} is the technical agricultural
title and ``ploughman'' lands closer to the colloquial mockery
Cicero is reaching for. The pun cannot survive in English.
\item \textbf{\S 24 \textit{quae vero profectio postea, quod
iter paludati, quae vitatio oculorum, lucis, urbis, fori, quam
misera fuga, quam foeda, quam turpis!}} The tricolon of
\textit{misera / foeda / turpis} closing a longer
\textit{quae vero $\ldots$ quod $\ldots$ quae $\ldots$}
catalogue of Antony's flight. Rendered ``what a setting out
afterwards, what a journey in the war-cloak, what avoiding of
men's eyes, of the light, of the City, of the Forum, what a
wretched flight, how foul, how disgraceful!'' The four
\textit{quae/quod} questions kept as four English ``what''s;
the closing tricolon preserved as ``how foul, how disgraceful''
sealing the longer build.
\item \textbf{\S 27 \textit{O praeclarum custodem ovium, ut
aiunt, lupum!}} The wolf-as-guardian-of-the-sheep aphorism.
Rendered ``O illustrious guardian of the sheep, as men say, a
wolf!'' The \textit{ut aiunt} (``as they say'') marks this as
a current proverb; preserved as ``as men say.'' The Plautine
flavour kept (cf.\ \textit{Pseudolus} 140 \textit{ovem
lupo committere}); worth an allusions entry once enrichment
runs.
\item \textbf{\S 28 \textit{hodierno die primum, patres
conscripti, longo intervallo in possessionem libertatis pedem
ponimus}.} The cadence opening the hortatory peroration.
Rendered ``On this day, senators, for the first time after a
long interval, we plant our foot upon the possession of
liberty.'' The legal-language \textit{in possessionem $\ldots$
pedem ponimus} (a phrase from possessory interdicts, ``we plant
our foot upon possession'') kept literal, since the legal
flavour is the load-bearing point: liberty has been won back as
property is won back, by entry-into-possession.
\item \textbf{\S 28 \textit{hanc vero taeterrimam beluam quis
ferre potest aut quo modo?}} Rendered ``But who can endure this
most foul beast --- and how?'' The \textit{taeterrima belua}
preserved as ``most foul beast'' (the metaphor is Cicero's
recurring one for Antony; ``creature'' or ``monster'' would
lose the load-bearing bestiality).
\item \textbf{\S 29 \textit{aut libertatem propriam Romani et
generis et nominis recuperemus aut mortem servituti
anteponamus}.} The choice. Rendered ``either let us recover
the liberty proper to the Roman race and name, or set death
before slavery.'' The \textit{aut $\ldots$ aut} construction
preserved as ``either $\ldots$ or''; \textit{anteponamus}
rendered ``set before'' rather than ``prefer'' to keep the
literal spatial image (death stationed in front of slavery as
a barrier).
\item \textbf{\S 31 \textit{ille autem ex myrmillone dux, ex
gladiatore imperator}.} Lucius Antonius the brother. Rendered
``that man, from murmillo a leader, from gladiator a
commander.'' Both \textit{myrmillo} (a specific gladiator class
with fish-crest helmet, sword, and shield) and \textit{gladiator}
kept in their Roman cultural specificity --- ``murmillo'' as a
loanword rather than naturalised to ``swordsman.'' The four
substantive nouns in the \textit{ex \ldots dux / ex \ldots
imperator} chiasmus preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 36 \textit{ad decus et ad libertatem nati
sumus: aut haec teneamus aut cum dignitate moriamur}.}
Rendered ``For glory and for liberty we were born: let us
either keep these, or die with dignity.'' The two
\textit{ad \ldots} prepositional phrases preserved as ``For
\ldots and for \ldots''; the closing \textit{aut \ldots aut}
echoes the \S 29 cadence and is preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 36 \textit{iucundiorem autem faciet
libertatem servitutis recordatio}.} The closing aphorism
before the formal motion. Rendered ``The recollection of
slavery will make liberty sweeter.'' \textit{Iucundiorem}
preserved as ``sweeter'' (rather than ``more pleasant''),
which lands the comparative and the affective weight
together.
\end{itemize}

sweep the major philosophical treatises (\textit{Cato Maior
De Senectute}, \textit{Paradoxa Stoicorum}, \textit{Academica},

rhetorica (\textit{partitiones-oratoriae}, \textit{brutus},

\textbf{Launch-prompt advisory for the scheduled 2-hour Cowork
task on Alexander's side:} the prompt text (\texttt{dispatch
3--4 parallel batches of worker sub-agents, each translating
3--5 short letters end-to-end}) remains stale and continues to
auto-pivot. Sessions 32--38 all dropped to single-substantial-
speech mode per this section's instructions; the same
auto-pivot will continue through the Philippic and
philosophical phases (sessions 39--60 or so). Updating the
scheduled-task prompt to reflect Phase 3 of OPERATING\_PLAN.md
(``one substantial-work worker per session, 5-section chunks,
WIP-emit discipline, multi-session splits allowed for very
long works'') is recommended but not blocking --- the PM in

and follows what it says.

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-37):} \textbf{907 /
958 works drafted (\~94.7\%)} --- session 37 completed
\textit{Philippic 2} (the two-session-split opened in session 36)
end-to-end. \textit{Philippic 2} sections 59--118 translated this
session, the headnote written, parallel sidecar regenerated
end-to-end across all 118 segments, and \texttt{status:} flipped
from \texttt{pending} to \texttt{drafted}. The chronological-gap
warning falls from \textbf{46} (post-session-36) to \textbf{45}
(post-session-37), exactly the expected decrement of one speech
moved out of the gap. \textbf{Latest drafted by deep chronology is
unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares} 10.24 (28 July 43 BC) remains
the marker, since \textit{Philippic 2}, even completed, is dated
October 44 BC and so falls inside the chronological-gap zone.

\textbf{Cowork session 37 --- 1 substantial speech completed
(1 worker, 60 sections (59--118), 5-section chunks):}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Worker A (\textit{Philippic 2} sections 59--118 ---
the second half of the never-delivered pamphlet of late October
44 BC, Cicero's most savage Caesarian-aftermath indictment):} 60
sections, $\sim$10{,}000 English words. Picks up at \S 59
(\textit{reliquorum factorum eius, patres conscripti, difficilis
est sane reprehensio et lubrica}) with the Brundisium return and
the ``great kindness'' of not killing Cicero, and runs through
\S 118 (the closing peroration's \textit{duo modo haec opto}:
that Cicero may die leaving the Roman people free, and that each
man may receive as he deserves of the commonwealth). \S 59--63
the second Italian tour, master-of-horse year, and the famous
Hippias-wedding vomiting scene (\textit{magister equitum, cui
ructare turpe esset, is vomens frustis esculentis vinum
redolentibus gremium suum et totum tribunal implevit}). \S 64--69
the purchase of Pompey's property at the post-Pharsalia auction:
the \textit{O audaciam immanem!} apostrophe of \S 68, the
\textit{Charybdis tam vorax} cadence of \S 66, the slaves' beds
spread with Pompey's purple coverlets (\S 67). \S 70--78 the
sham auction list, the Spanish-war absence, the comic
Narbo-and-back midnight ride in Gallic slippers and a cloak with
the lover's letter for Fulvia (\S 77 \textit{O hominem nequam!}).
\S 79--84 the false-augury Dolabella set-piece, with the famous
\textit{`Alio die' inquit} of \S 83 (the augur announcing the
flaw after the comitia had run their course). \S 85--87 the
great Lupercalia diadem-offering scene (\S 85 \textit{sedebat
in rostris collega tuus amictus toga purpurea, in sella aurea,
coronatus}; the diadem brought from home, \textit{meditatum et
cogitatum scelus}; \S 86 \textit{O praeclaram illam eloquentiam
tuam, cum es nudus contionatus!}). \S 88--92 the Ides aftermath
--- Antony's flight, the funeral oration that loosed the mob
(\S 91 \textit{tu, tu, inquam, illas faces incendisti}), the
magnificent abolition of the dictatorship shadowed at once by
the huckstering of immunities at the temple of Ops. \S 93--99
the forged Caesarian decrees (Deiotarus syngrapha, Crete from
tribute, exile-recalls), with the savage \S 99 attack on
Antony's treatment of his uncle Lucius Caesar and of Antonia.
\S 100--108 the Italian tour with mimes and gamblers on the
Campanian and Leontine land, the desecration of Varro's villa
at Casinum (\S 104 \textit{personabant omnia vocibus ebriorum,
natabant pavimenta vino, madebant parietes}), the return to
Rome with armed guards (\S 108 \textit{agmine quadrato cum
gladiis sequuntur}). \S 109--112 demand to know why Antony as
Caesar's flamen neglects the praetexta, why Ituraeans are in
the Forum, why the doors of Concord are not open (\S 112
\textit{pax est tranquilla libertas}, the famous definition).
\S 113--118 the great peroration: the Liberators' example
(\S 114), Antony's wife and her \textit{tertia pensio}
(\S 113), the closing offer of Cicero's own body (\S 117
\textit{defendi rem publicam adulescens, non deseram senex:
contempsi Catilinae gladios, non pertimescam tuos}), the prayer
\textit{ut moriens populum Romanum liberum relinquam} (\S 118).
\textbf{Sidecar:} 60-segment parallel-sidecar append in lockstep
with the existing 58 (re-emitted end-to-end at session close);
no Greek phrases in this half; no date correction needed
(\texttt{-0044-10-01} month-precision placeholder still correct).
\textbf{Headnote:} four paragraphs covering composition history
(the never-delivered pamphlet, sent to Atticus 25 October per
\textit{Ad Atticum} 15.13a, 16.11, circulated through November),
the two-movement structure (defence \S 1--41 / counter-attack
\S 42--58 / indictment \S 59--118), and the prophetic close
(Cicero killed at Formiae 7 December 43 BC, head and hands
nailed to the Rostra).
\end{itemize}

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 37.}
The meta entry was already correct after session 36.

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 63 \textit{magister equitum, cui ructare turpe
esset, is vomens frustis esculentis vinum redolentibus}.}
Rendered ``master of horse, for whom even a belch would be a
disgrace, this man, vomiting, filled his own lap and the whole
tribunal with bits of food reeking of wine.'' Preserved the
\textit{ructare turpe esset} contrast as a stronger ``even a
belch would be a disgrace'' --- the load-bearing point is that
the lesser disgrace is intolerable and the greater has been
committed.
\item \textbf{\S 65 \textit{`male parta male dilabuntur.'}}
Cicero quotes a poet (probably Naevius, perhaps Ennius ---
the source is unrecovered). Rendered ``Ill gained, ill
spent.'' Preserved as a quotation in inverted commas, the
alliterative shape of \textit{male/male} kept by ``ill/ill.''
Worth an allusions entry once enrichment runs.
\item \textbf{\S 66 \textit{quae Charybdis tam vorax?}} The
famous Charybdis cadence. Rendered ``What Charybdis was ever
so voracious?'' Preserved the proper name (Homeric monster)
and the \textit{tam vorax} as ``so voracious.'' \S 67
continues the conceit with \textit{Oceanus, me dius fidius,
vix videtur tot res tam dissipatas \ldots tam cito absorbere
potuisse} --- ``Ocean itself, by Hercules, scarcely seems
\ldots so quickly to engulf'' --- \textit{me dius fidius}
kept as ``by Hercules'' per STYLE.md.
\item \textbf{\S 68 \textit{O audaciam immanem!}} Rendered ``O
monstrous audacity!'' --- \textit{immanis} preserved as
``monstrous'' (the standard force; ``immense'' or ``huge''
would lose the moral weight).
\item \textbf{\S 71 \textit{gustaras civilem sanguinem vel
potius exsorbueras}.} Rendered ``You had tasted citizen blood,
or rather drained it down.'' The two verbs are deliberately
graded: \textit{gustare} (taste) modified to \textit{exsorbere}
(drink to the dregs). Preserved as ``tasted \ldots drained
down.''
\item \textbf{\S 77 \textit{ergo, ut te catamitum, nec opinato
cum te ostendisses, praeter spem mulier aspiceret}.} Rendered
``So, to make a woman set eyes upon you, my catamite,
unhoped-for, when you showed yourself unexpectedly.'' Preserved
\textit{catamitum} as ``catamite'' (the Latin is the unrestrained
direct insult; ``minion'' or ``favourite'' would weaken).
\item \textbf{\S 83 \textit{`Alio die' inquit}.} The famous
augur's announcement after the comitia had run their course.
Rendered ``says: `On another day.'\,'' --- the technical
augural formula kept in inverted commas as an English
equivalent.
\item \textbf{\S 85 \textit{sedebat in rostris collega tuus
amictus toga purpurea, in sella aurea, coronatus}.} The
Lupercalia set-piece opening. Rendered ``Your colleague was
sitting on the Rostra, wrapped in a purple toga, on a golden
chair, crowned.'' The four-part description \textit{in rostris
/ amictus toga purpurea / in sella aurea / coronatus} preserved
as four short members in the English.
\item \textbf{\S 85 \textit{attuleras domo meditatum et
cogitatum scelus}.} Rendered ``you had brought it from home, a
crime premeditated and considered.'' The \textit{meditatum et
cogitatum} doublet preserved (premeditation is the load-bearing
point: the diadem was not a spontaneous Lupercalian flourish).
\item \textbf{\S 86 \textit{O praeclaram illam eloquentiam
tuam, cum es nudus contionatus!}} Rendered ``O that splendid
eloquence of yours, when you spoke in a public meeting naked!''
The \textit{nudus contionatus} is the famous image (Antony as
Lupercus stripped to the loincloth, addressing the assembly);
kept literal. The point will return at \S 111 (\textit{tuum
hominis simplicis pectus vidimus}).
\item \textbf{\S 91 \textit{tu, tu, inquam, illas faces
incendisti}.} The repeated \textit{tu, tu} drives the cadence.
Rendered ``you, you, I say, lit those torches.'' The repetition
preserved; \textit{illas faces} as ``those torches.''
\item \textbf{\S 99 \textit{quis interpretari potest,
impudentiorne qui in senatu, an improbior qui in Dolabellam, an
impurior qui patre audiente, an crudelior qui in illam miseram
tam spurce, tam impie dixeris}.} Four-part \textit{impudentior
\ldots improbior \ldots impurior \ldots crudelior} cadence,
the four-part-question rhetorical figure Cicero uses often.
Rendered as four parallel ``who-clauses'' (``Who can interpret
whether you were more impudent in saying it in the Senate, or
more depraved in saying it against Dolabella, or fouler in
saying it before her father's hearing, or more cruel against
that wretched woman in speaking so filthily, so impiously'').
\item \textbf{\S 104 \textit{personabant omnia vocibus ebriorum,
natabant pavimenta vino, madebant parietes}.} The famous
Casinum-desecration tricolon. Rendered ``all the place echoed
with the voices of drunkards, the pavements swam with wine,
the walls were soaked.'' The three verbs \textit{personabant /
natabant / madebant} preserved as three short members
(``echoed \ldots swam \ldots were soaked'').
\item \textbf{\S 108 \textit{agmine quadrato cum gladiis
sequuntur; scutorum lecticas portari videmus}.} Rendered
``They follow in a hollow square with their swords; we see
litters borne by shield-bearers.'' \textit{Agmine quadrato} is
the technical military formation kept as ``hollow square'';
\textit{scutorum lecticas} (``litters of shield-bearers'')
preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 112 \textit{pax est tranquilla libertas,
servitus postremum malorum omnium, non modo bello sed morte
etiam repellendum}.} The famous definition. Rendered ``Peace
is tranquil liberty, slavery the last of all evils, to be
repelled not only by war but even by death.'' The
\textit{tranquilla libertas} kept literal; the
\textit{non modo \ldots sed \ldots etiam} construction preserved
in the English.
\item \textbf{\S 113 \textit{quae se adhuc tantum modo ulta
est, nondum recuperavit}.} The commonwealth that has avenged
itself but not yet recovered itself. Rendered ``which thus far
has only avenged itself, has not yet recovered itself.'' The
load-bearing antithesis \textit{ulta / recuperavit} preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 114 \textit{exemplum facti reliquerunt. illi
quod nemo fecerat fecerunt}.} The two short balanced clauses
on the Liberators' example. Rendered ``they have left the
example of the deed. They did what no one had done.'' The
\textit{quod nemo fecerat fecerunt} polyptoton kept as ``what
no one had done. They did.''
\item \textbf{\S 117 \textit{defendi rem publicam adulescens,
non deseram senex; contempsi Catilinae gladios, non
pertimescam tuos}.} The two-part biographical-rhetorical
cadence at the climax. Rendered ``I defended the commonwealth
as a young man, I shall not desert it as an old man: I
despised the swords of Catiline, I shall not be afraid of
yours.'' The parallel \textit{adulescens/senex} and
\textit{Catilinae/tuos} antitheses preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 118 \textit{etenim si abhinc annos prope
viginti hoc ipso in templo negavi posse mortem immaturam esse
consulari, quanto verius nunc negabo seni!}} The famous
self-quotation back to \textit{In Catilinam} 4 (December 63
BC, twenty years before). Rendered ``For if nearly twenty
years ago in this very temple I declared that death could not
come untimely to a consular, how much more truly shall I now
declare it cannot come so to an old man!'' Worth a crossrefs
entry to \textit{In Catilinam} 4 (the Senate of 5 December 63
BC was also in the temple of Concord) once enrichment runs.
\item \textbf{\S 118 \textit{ut moriens populum Romanum liberum
relinquam}.} The closing prayer. Rendered ``that dying I may
leave the Roman people free.'' Preserved the participial
\textit{moriens} as ``dying'' rather than expanding to ``at
my death''; the bare participial has the prophetic-cadence
weight that Cicero (and the reader looking ahead to 7 December
43 BC) is reaching for.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Suggested next translation batch} (when session 37 is
landed via \texttt{bash scripts/cowork\_handoff.sh "session 37:
\ldots"} and session 38 opens):

\textbf{Session 38 opens \textit{Philippic 3} (20 December 44
BC, $\sim$39 sections).} The first Philippic delivered after
Octavian's break with Antony --- the Senate is summoned by
the tribunes on 20 December to consider the new situation
(Octavian has the legions of Mars and IV at Alba; Decimus
Brutus has refused to surrender Cisalpine Gaul; Antony is
marching to take it). The speech praises Octavian, Decimus
Brutus, and the deserting legions, and proposes that Antony
be declared a public enemy in all but the name (the actual
\textit{hostis} declaration would not come until April 43).
Single-substantial-speech pattern, 1 session: fetch via
\texttt{scripts/fetch\_latin.py philippic-3}, translate in
5-section chunks with parallel-sidecar lockstep emission and
headnote at close.

\textit{Philippic 4} (20 Dec 44, $\sim$16 sections, same day
as Phil 3, the speech to the people of the same business),
\textit{5} (1 Jan 43, $\sim$53 sections), \textit{6} (4 Jan
43, $\sim$19 sections), \textit{7} (Jan 43, $\sim$27
sections), \textit{8} (3 Feb 43, $\sim$33 sections),
\textit{9} (4 Feb 43, $\sim$17 sections), \textit{10} (Feb
43, $\sim$26 sections), \textit{11} (Mar 43, $\sim$40
sections), \textit{12} (Mar 43, $\sim$30 sections),
\textit{13} (20 Mar 43, $\sim$50 sections), \textit{14} (21
Apr 43, $\sim$38 sections). Each fits a single session;
total Philippic phase is likely 12 sessions left (Phil 2
just completed, Phil 3--14 to go).

\textbf{After the Philippics} (sessions $\sim$50--60 or so):

ending at the famous Helen-of-Troy cadence \textit{Vt Helena

\textbf{Cowork session 36 --- 1 substantial speech opened, 1 of 2
sessions done (1 worker, 58 of 118 sections, 5-section chunks):}

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Worker A (\textit{Philippic 2} sections 1--58 ---
the never-delivered written pamphlet of late October 44 BC,
Cicero's most famous invective against Antony, circulated as a
pamphlet because never spoken aloud in the Senate; first half
translated end-to-end):} 58 sections, $\sim$9{,}200 English
words. The opening \S 1--10 are Cicero's direct response to
Antony's invective speech of 19 September: the augurate
canvass, the Brundisium ``mercy,'' the friend's-letter that
Antony read aloud to the Senate (\textit{quis enim umquam qui
paulum modo bonorum consuetudinem nosset, litteras ad se ab
amico missas \ldots palamque recitavit?}). \S 11--19 defend
Cicero's consulship and the suppression of Catiline (with the
\S 12 roll-call of consulars who approved it, and the \S 14--15
contrast of Antony's uncle Lucius Caesar with Antony himself ---
\textit{vitrici te similem quam avunculi maluisti}). \S 20--36
turn to the Ides-of-March charge: did Cicero counsel the
killing? With the \S 28 \textit{cruentum alte extollens Brutus
pugionem Ciceronem nominatim exclamavit} set-piece, the \S 30
\textit{edormi crapulam, inquam, et exhala} (``sleep off the
drunkenness, I say, and breathe it out''), the \S 32 \textit{in
equum Troianum cum principibus includis} self-acceptance into
the conspirators' number, and the \S 34 brilliant counter-attack
\textit{quem et Narbone hoc consilium cum C. Trebonio cepisse
notissimum est} --- Antony himself once thought of killing
Caesar at Narbo. \S 37--41 dispatch the camp-of-Pompey and
legacies charges, with the \S 40 \textit{sestertium ducentiens
acceptum hereditatibus rettuli} and the \S 41 Lucius Turselius
joke (``Tell me, please \ldots Lucius Turselius --- what was
his face, what his stature, of what municipality, of what
tribe?''). \S 42--46 are the savage Curio episode --- Antony's
youthful bankruptcy, the prostitute-then-matron \textit{stola}
metaphor (\textit{tamquam stolam dedisset, in matrimonio
stabili et certo conlocavit}), and Cicero's account of being
begged by the younger Curio to defend him against his father
over the six-million-sesterce bond. \S 47--50 sketch the
Alexandria-with-Gabinius episode and Antony's quaestorship.
\S 51--55 are the seed-of-civil-war argument: Antony's tribunate
veto of January 49 BC gave Caesar his pretext, and \textit{Vt
Helena Troianis, sic iste huic rei publicae belli causa, causa
pestis atque exiti fuit} (\S 55). \S 56 is the dicing-law
restoration of Licinius Lenticula (Antony's gambling crony),
\S 57--58 the lictors-and-mime tour through the Italian
municipalities. \textbf{Sidecar:} 58-segment parallel sidecar
emitted in lockstep, no Greek phrases in this half, no date
correction needed (the \texttt{-0044-10-01} month-precision
placeholder is correct; the speech was written and circulated
in late October/early November but never delivered). \textbf{No
headnote yet}: per the multi-session pattern, the headnote will
be written by session 37 once the speech is complete and the
status is flipped.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{No metadata corrections committed during session 36.}
The meta entry's date, Perseus URL (\texttt{phi0474.phi035} with
\texttt{speech\_index: '2'}), and section count all matched the
fetched Latin (118 sections in the Perseus TEI, exactly).

\textbf{Translator-notes worth logging in
\texttt{data/translator-notes.jsonl}} (Cowork did not write the
shared jsonl directly to avoid concurrent-write contention;
deferred to a future enrichment pass):
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{\S 7 \textit{cum uno gladiatore nequissimo}.}
Rendered ``a single worthless gladiator''; kept the contempt of
\textit{gladiator} (the standard Roman political-invective
slur, not literal) rather than naturalising.
\item \textbf{\S 8 \textit{ut mustelae tamen Seio et Tironi
Numisio videris}.} Kept the weasel image (``you nevertheless
appear like a weasel'') --- the Latin uses an obscure private
nickname; \textit{mustela} as ``weasel'' is the standard force
and preserves the contempt without naturalising.
\item \textbf{\S 14 \textit{ad eos scilicet quorum nobis etiam
dies natales audiendi sunt}.} Rendered ``those of course whose
very birthdays we have to hear announced'' --- the contempt is
in the public listing of the birthdays of low-born intimates;
preserved \textit{scilicet} as the sneering ``of course.''
\item \textbf{\S 15 \textit{O foeditatem hominis flagitiosam}.}
Asseverative tricolon rendered ``O the foul and disgraceful
character of the man, his unbearable shamelessness,
worthlessness, lust!'' --- the \textit{O} interjection kept;
the four nouns \textit{foeditatem, impudentiam, nequitiam,
libidinem} preserved as a four-part cadence (the speech is full
of these).
\item \textbf{\S 20 \textit{`cedant arma togae'}.} Cicero
quotes his own famous line from \textit{De Consulatu Suo}.
Rendered ``Let arms yield to the toga.'' Worth a crossrefs
entry to \textit{De Consulatu Suo} once enrichment runs ---
this is one of Cicero's most famously-mocked self-quotations.
\item \textbf{\S 27 \textit{depulsorque dominatus quam particeps
esse maluit}.} Rendered ``chose rather to be the overthrower
of despotism than to share in it'' --- the contrast between
\textit{depulsor} and \textit{particeps} is the load-bearing
antithesis; preserved.
\item \textbf{\S 28 \textit{me potissimum testatus est se
aemulum mearum laudum exstitisse}.} Cicero's self-flattery
crux: rendered ``called me as the most fitting witness that he
had stood forth as a rival of my fame.'' Preserved
\textit{aemulum mearum laudum} (``a rival of my fame'') rather
than weakening to ``imitator'' or ``follower'' --- the audacity
of the equation Brutus-with-Cicero-the-Catiline-suppressor is
the point.
\item \textbf{\S 30 \textit{edormi crapulam, inquam, et
exhala}.} Rendered ``Sleep off the drunkenness, I say, and
breathe it out.'' \textit{Crapulam} kept as ``drunkenness''
rather than ``hangover''; the \textit{exhala} continues the
breath/wine image that Cicero will return to in \S 42
(\textit{vini exhalandi}) and \S 63 (the famous Hippias-wedding
vomiting scene).
\item \textbf{\S 32 \textit{in equum Troianum cum principibus
includis}.} Rendered ``into the Trojan horse \ldots enclose me
with the chief men'' --- preserved the simile in full; the
\textit{principes} are the chief conspirators, the Brutuses
and Cassius, whom Antony has already named in honour.
\item \textbf{\S 33 four-part \textit{adfari atque appetere /
agrestes \ldots / immemor posteritas \ldots / ingratae
litterae}.} Cadence preserved as four parallel rhetorical
questions, each opening with the relative-clause shape
(``What place \ldots What men \ldots What posterity \ldots
what books \ldots'').
\item \textbf{\S 34 \textit{si meus stilus ille fuisset, ut
dicitur, mihi crede, non solum unum actum sed totam fabulam
confecissem}.} The famous \textit{stilus}-as-stylus-and-as-dagger
double sense, the joke that Cicero would have killed not only
Caesar but the whole following ``act'' (Antony). Rendered
``if that pen had been mine that men say was, then, believe me,
I should have finished not only the one act but the whole
play.'' The dramatic-play metaphor preserved cleanly.
\item \textbf{\S 41 \textit{velim mihi dicas, nisi molestum est,
L. Turselius qua facie fuerit, qua statura, quo municipio, qua
tribu}.} The famous Turselius joke. Rendered as a polite query
list (``Tell me, please, if it is not too much trouble: Lucius
Turselius --- what was his face, what his stature, of what
municipality, of what tribe?'') --- preserving the \textit{nisi
molestum est} courtesy and the four parallel ablatives.
\item \textbf{\S 44 \textit{sumpsisti virilem, quam statim
muliebrem togam reddidisti}.} The toga \textit{virilis} /
\textit{muliebris} distinction is the load-bearing point: the
boy's transition-to-manhood toga turned overnight into the
prostitute-courtesan stola. Rendered ``You took on the man's
toga, which you straightway turned into a woman's.''
\item \textbf{\S 44 \textit{tamquam stolam dedisset, in
matrimonio stabili et certo conlocavit}.} Curio is figured as
the husband, Antony as the wife. Rendered ``as if he had given
you a matron's stole, settled you in a fixed and steady
marriage.'' The matron's \textit{stola} kept as ``matron's
stole'' to preserve the Roman dress-code reference; the
metaphor is one of the most famously obscene in Cicero.
\item \textbf{\S 48 \textit{Suam enim quisque domum tum
obtinebat nec erat usquam tua}.} Rendered ``every man at that
time had his own house, but you had none anywhere.'' Kept the
\textit{usquam} as ``anywhere'' for the totality.
\item \textbf{\S 51 \textit{quod in hostem togatum decerni est
solitum more maiorum}.} Rendered ``what is usually decreed in
the manner of our ancestors against an enemy in the toga.''
\textit{Hostem togatum} kept as ``enemy in the toga'' rather
than collapsing --- it is the technical contrast (a citizen,
not a foreign enemy, declared the state's enemy).
\item \textbf{\S 55 \textit{Vt Helena Troianis, sic iste huic
rei publicae belli causa, causa pestis atque exiti fuit}.}
The famous Helen-of-Troy comparison: Antony is to the
commonwealth what Helen was to Troy. Rendered ``As Helen was
to the Trojans, so was this man to this commonwealth, the
cause of war, the cause of pestilence and ruin.'' Preserved
the doubled \textit{causa \ldots causa} cadence; worth a
crossrefs/allusions entry once enrichment runs (the figure is
Homeric-tragic and would have been instantly recognised).
\item \textbf{\S 58 \textit{aperta lectica mima portabatur,
quam \ldots non noto illo et mimico nomine, sed Volumniam
consalutabant}.} The townspeople greet Antony's mime-actress
mistress not by her stage-name (Cytheris) but by her freedwoman
\textit{nomen} Volumnia, as if she were a respectable matron.
Rendered ``greeted not by her well-known and theatrical name,
but as Volumnia.'' The crux preserved without naturalising.
\end{itemize}

\textbf{Suggested next translation batch} (when session 36 is
landed via \texttt{bash scripts/cowork\_handoff.sh "session 36:
\ldots"} and session 37 opens):

\textbf{Session 37 finishes \textit{Philippic 2}.} Pick up from
\S 59 (\textit{reliquorum factorum eius, patres conscripti,
difficilis est sane reprehensio et lubrica}) and translate
through \S 118. The Latin is already in
\texttt{latin/speeches/044bc-philippic-2.tex} (fetched at
session 36 start); the existing English file should be
\textbf{appended} (not overwritten) starting from \S 59 ---
strip the trailing \texttt{\% --- End of session 36's first half}
comment block first, then continue. The parallel-sidecar JSON
will need to be regenerated end-to-end at session 37's close
(the included Python parser used in session 36 reads both files
and matches by section, so re-running it picks up the new
sections automatically). \textbf{Session 37 also writes the
headnote} (\texttt{headnotes/speeches/044bc-philippic-2.tex})
and flips \texttt{status:} from \texttt{pending} to
\texttt{drafted} in \texttt{meta/works.yaml}. The chronological-
gap warning will fall from 46 to 45 at that point.

The Phil 2 second half contains the major set-pieces:
\textbf{\S 59--62} Brundisium return and the second Italian
tour with the mime; \textbf{\S 63} the famous Hippias-wedding
vomiting scene (\textit{in coetu vero populi Romani negotium
publicum gerens, magister equitum, cui ructare turpe esset, is
vomens frustis esculentis vinum redolentibus gremium suum et
totum tribunal implevit}); \textbf{\S 64--69} the purchase of
Pompey's property at the post-Pharsalia auction (the famous
\textit{O audaciam immanem! tu etiam ingredi illam domum ausus
es} apostrophe and the \textit{Charybdis tam vorax} cadence of
\S 67); \textbf{\S 70--84} the master-of-horse year, the Italian
tour, the assassination-attempt at the Tarsalia camp, and the
\textit{Cn. Pompei sector}; \textbf{\S 85--87} the Lupercalia
diadem-offering set-piece (\textit{sedebat in rostris collega
tuus amictus toga purpurea, in sella aurea, coronatus} and
Cicero's interrogation: \textit{cur diadema imposuisti?});
\textbf{\S 88--92} the false augury at the Forum auction of
Caesar's effects; \textbf{\S 93--108} the great peroration ---
Antony's foreign-policy disasters, the Brundisium-mutiny
murders, the Mutina build-up; and \textbf{\S 109--118} the
final apostrophe to Antony asking him to recall the example of
Caesar's end (\textit{cogita, quaeso, M. Antoni, etiam id
quemadmodum recipias quod ego de re publica sentio}) and the
closing peroration on the speaker's own willingness to die for
the commonwealth (\textit{etenim si abhinc annos prope viginti
hoc ipso in templo negavi posse mortem immaturam esse
consulari, quanto verius nunc negabo seni!}). The second half
is slightly longer than the first ($\sim$60 vs.\ 58 sections,
likely $\sim$9.5--10K English words), but well within a single
session's budget on the single-substantial-speech pattern.

sections). Each fits a single session; total Philippic phase is
likely 13 sessions (1 done, 1 in progress, 12 to go counting
session 37 as completing Phil 2 and starting Phil 3).

\textbf{After the Philippics} (sessions $\sim$48--58 or so):
sweep the major philosophical treatises (\textit{Cato Maior De

\textbf{Translation state (post-Cowork-session-35):} \textbf{906 /
958 works drafted (\~94.6\%)}. \textbf{Latest drafted by deep
chronology is unchanged}: \textit{Ad Familiares} 10.24 (Plancus's
camp in Gaul, 28 July 43 BC) remains the marker, because the one
speech drafted this session (\textit{Philippic 1}, 2 September 44
BC) is earlier than the marker. Session 35 \textbf{opened the
Philippic phase} that PROGRESS.md post-session-34 signposted: one
worker, end-to-end translation of the first of the fourteen
Philippics, with 5-section-per-Edit chunk discipline and parallel-
